A large, strong upper level trough approached far west Texas from the west. In response, a deep surface trough developed in the southern plains to the northeast of the area. The tight surface pressure gradient and mixing down of strong winds aloft resulted in high winds at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were measured from 59 mph to 73 mph across the northeast and east portions of El Paso from the evening of the 13th through the noon hour of the 14th. A roof was blown off of a house in northeast El Paso. Also, several power lines blown down resulting in power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shallow arctic airmass moved into the region a few days earlier resulting in near to slightly below freezing temperatures across portions of northeast Texas.  A warm...moist layer of air overran the shallower, colder air at the surface with patchy light drizzle and light rain falling across the region.  The drizzle and light rain became freezing drizzle and light freezing rain across portions of northeast Texas during the predawn hours of December 23rd.  The ice froze to elevated bridges and overpasses which in turn, resulted in several vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive upper atmospheric storm system quickly passed over the southern Rockies during the afternoon hours of the 18th. A deepening surface low pressure area over southeastern Colorado and southwestern Kansas in advance of this system and a strong lower level wind maximum resulted in widespread sustained winds in the 25 to 35 mph range over the western Texas Panhandle and South Plains. A localized severe gust of 62 mph was measured by the KVII-TV Schoolnet at Friona (Parmer County) shortly after 14:30 CST. No damage was reported.

A shallow but very cold artic airmass spilled southward into the Southern Plains into the Lower Mississippi Valley. This airmass resulted in surface temperatures hovering near to slightly below freezing across portions of northeast Texas.  A warm...moist airmass overran this shallow, colder air near the surface with light rain developing across the county. The light rain became freezing rain across portions of the region with ice accumulations below one quarter of an inch reported.  The ice collected on trees and power lines as well as elevated bridges and overpasses. There were no power disruptions but a few minor accidents were reported across portions of northeast Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icing was reported on bridges and overpasses on State Highway 82, State Highway 37 and U.S. 271.  Ice was also reported on the loop in Clarksville, Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across Deep South Texas and the Lower Rio Grande Valley during the overnight and early morning of December 9th and 10th, bringing periods of sustained gale force winds to the Laguna Madre and adjacent Gulf of Mexico waters out 20 nm, as well as a prolonged period of sustained winds of 30 mph with frequent gusts between above 45 mph, generally between 9 PM on the 9th and 6 AM on the 10th, most pronounced near the coast.||Behind the front, temperatures struggled through the 50s despite ample sunshine on the 10th, setting the stage for the first freeze of the young winter the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds allowed temperatures to plummet across mainly rural northern Hidalgo County during the morning of December 11.  At the Linn-San Manuel Mesowest (RAWS) observing station, part of the Lower Rio Grande Valley National Wildlife Refuge, the temperature fell to 30 degrees just after 7 AM.  While this location is just over the county line in extreme northwest Willacy County, interpolation between this location and the Falfurrias, TX (Brooks County) cooperative station argue for at least two hours of 32 degree F or lower readings, generally north of an Edinburg-Alton line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across Deep South Texas and the Lower Rio Grande Valley during the overnight and early morning of December 9th and 10th, bringing periods of sustained gale force winds to the Laguna Madre and adjacent Gulf of Mexico waters out 20 nm, as well as a prolonged period of sustained winds of 30 mph with frequent gusts between above 45 mph, generally between 9 PM on the 9th and 6 AM on the 10th, most pronounced near the coast.||Behind the front, temperatures struggled through the 50s despite ample sunshine on the 10th, setting the stage for the first freeze of the young winter the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds allowed temperatures to plummet across mainly rural central and southern Jim Hogg County during the morning of December 11.  While temperatures at higher terrain, generally above 500 feet, may not have reached freezing, lower elevations likely fell to or below 32 for at least two hours just prior to sunrise, based on interpolation between similar cooperative observations at least a cooperative station (Brooks County, to the east), and one airport location (Zapata County, to the west).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During December, drought conditions persisted across South Texas, and even intensified in some areas. By the end of December, extreme drought conditions existed across Goliad and Victoria counties. Severe drought conditions existed across Live Oak, Bee, Refugio, Aransas, and San Patricio counties. Moderate drought existed across Calhoun, McMullen, La Salle, Nueces, northern Jim Wells and northern Duval counties. Abnormally dry conditions were observed across Webb, Kleberg, southern Jim Wells and southern Duval counties. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor:  http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html)||The primary impacts of the drought during December continued to be agricultural in drought counties. Forage was sparse and the supplemental feeding of hay to livestock continued.  The fire danger threat also increased during the month, especially with the use of fireworks with the approach of New Years. In addition, reservoir levels continued a gradual fall. Rivers and creeks in the rainfall deficit areas continued a slow fall, and were below baseflow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During December, drought conditions persisted across South Texas, and even intensified in some areas. By the end of December, extreme drought conditions existed across Goliad and Victoria counties. Severe drought conditions existed across Live Oak, Bee, Refugio, Aransas, and San Patricio counties. Moderate drought existed across Calhoun, McMullen, La Salle, Nueces, northern Jim Wells and northern Duval counties. Abnormally dry conditions were observed across Webb, Kleberg, southern Jim Wells and southern Duval counties. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor:  http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html)||The primary impacts of the drought during December continued to be agricultural in drought counties. Forage was sparse and the supplemental feeding of hay to livestock continued.  The fire danger threat also increased during the month, especially with the use of fireworks with the approach of New Years. In addition, reservoir levels continued a gradual fall. Rivers and creeks in the rainfall deficit areas continued a slow fall, and were below baseflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through northern Texas during the mid to late morning of the 29th. Behind the front, extremely strong wind gusts were reported over much of the area. Numerous locations reported wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were reported in isolated locations. Minor damage was repored, but no injuries occurred. Monetary damages were estimated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The non-thunderstorm wind gust was reported 3 miles west of Goodlett.

At the Falfurrias, TX, cooperative station, located at the Hollywood Hunting Camp just west of town, the low fell to 28 degrees. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across Deep South Texas and the Lower Rio Grande Valley during the overnight and early morning of December 9th and 10th, bringing periods of sustained gale force winds to the Laguna Madre and adjacent Gulf of Mexico waters out 20 nm, as well as a prolonged period of sustained winds of 30 mph with frequent gusts between above 45 mph, generally between 9 PM on the 9th and 6 AM on the 10th, most pronounced near the coast.||Behind the front, temperatures struggled through the 50s despite ample sunshine on the 10th, setting the stage for the first freeze of the young winter the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds allowed temperatures to plummet across large portions of the King Ranch in Kenedy County during the morning of December 11.  While two cooperative stations are normally cooler than most, even these stations were significantly cold enough to justify a more widespread freeze.  At the retreat 7 miles east of Sarita, a record low for the date of 25F was set, and the observing post at Armstrong, about 20 miles to the south, dipped below 30F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dry line developed over north central Texas on the afternoon of February 25th and began moving eastward in conjunction with an upper level trough moving through the mid-Mississippi Valley.  Moderate lapse rates and lift associated with the dry line helped spur a line of storms in the late afternoon hours over northeast Texas and southwestern Arkansas.  The storms marched eastward throughout the evening and produced hail mainly under 1 inch in many locations across east Texas, southern Arkansas, and northwest Louisiana with some wind damage reported in southern Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell for about two minutes covering the ground.

A strong upper level low pressure system combined with surface temperatures near the freezing mark resulted in  the development of severe thunderstorms across the Big Country that produced large hail.  At times, the large hail occurred simultaneously with the freezing rain.

A strong upper level storm system moved from the southwest United States and into the southern plains during the day. A very unstable airmass was noted across the four state region with abundant low level wind shear and deep layer shear in the mid levels of the atmosphere. A dry line moved into southeast Oklahoma and northeast Texas by late afternoon with scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms developing ahead of the dry line across the region. Thunderstorms produced strong and damaging wind gusts along with large hail across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell on Hwy 344 west, 2 miles east of Hwy 155.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved from the southwest United States and into the southern plains during the day. A very unstable airmass was noted across the four state region with abundant low level wind shear and deep layer shear in the mid levels of the atmosphere. A dry line moved into southeast Oklahoma and northeast Texas by late afternoon with scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms developing ahead of the dry line across the region. Thunderstorms produced strong and damaging wind gusts along with large hail across most of the region.

Persistent dry weather over the summer into the fall has resulted in severe drought conditions across the Northwest Hill Country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions (D2) which began in September continued into October.  Range and pasture conditions were extremely poor due to the dry conditions.  Supplemental feeding of livestock has also increased. Stock tank levels continued to drop.  The dry weather has also created fire weather concerns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area tracekd across east Texas and western Louisiana, creating up to 8 inches of rainfall. This resulted in some flash flooding in the Jasper, TX area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes on Second Street had water enter them after as much as eight inches of rain fell in a short time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During December, drought conditions persisted across South Texas, and even intensified in some areas. By the end of December, extreme drought conditions existed across Goliad and Victoria counties. Severe drought conditions existed across Live Oak, Bee, Refugio, Aransas, and San Patricio counties. Moderate drought existed across Calhoun, McMullen, La Salle, Nueces, northern Jim Wells and northern Duval counties. Abnormally dry conditions were observed across Webb, Kleberg, southern Jim Wells and southern Duval counties. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor:  http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html)||The primary impacts of the drought during December continued to be agricultural in drought counties. Forage was sparse and the supplemental feeding of hay to livestock continued.  The fire danger threat also increased during the month, especially with the use of fireworks with the approach of New Years. In addition, reservoir levels continued a gradual fall. Rivers and creeks in the rainfall deficit areas continued a slow fall, and were below baseflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dry line developed over north central Texas on the afternoon of February 25th and began moving eastward in conjunction with an upper level trough moving through the mid-Mississippi Valley.  Moderate lapse rates and lift associated with the dry line helped spur a line of storms in the late afternoon hours over northeast Texas and southwestern Arkansas.  The storms marched eastward throughout the evening and produced hail mainly under 1 inch in many locations across east Texas, southern Arkansas, and northwest Louisiana with some wind damage reported in southern Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in a moist and unstable atmosphere.  A storm collapsed and produced isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were broken along with several fences blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short bowing line segment moved across La Salle and Webb Counties into McMullen and Duval Counties, producing straight line wind damage. The line slowly weakened as it moved east into a more stable airmass across the southern Coastal Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree limbs were snapped and a few trees completely uprooted.  Several power poles were blown down.  At a high school, stadium lights were damaged and a dugout roof was ripped off at the baseball field. The high school roof auditorium was also damaged.  Part of a gymnasium roof was also torn off.  A NWS storm survey of the damage in Benavides revealed a divergent, downburst wind pattern about 1 mile wide and 3 miles long on the southwest side of the town, near Highway 359 and 339.  Damage was mainly to trees, metal roofs and weaker, west facing structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short bowing line segment moved across La Salle and Webb Counties into McMullen and Duval Counties, producing straight line wind damage. The line slowly weakened as it moved east into a more stable airmass across the southern Coastal Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted on F.M. 735, near Palito Blanco.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow fell in Pottsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During November, drought conditions expanded southwest across South Texas. By the end of November, severe drought conditions existed across Bee, Goliad, Victoria, Refugio, Aransas, and San Patricio counties. Moderate drought existed across Calhoun, Live Oak, McMullen, La Salle, Nueces, northern Jim Wells and northern Duval counties by the end of November. Abnormally dry conditions were observed across Webb, Kleberg, southern Jim Wells and southern Duval counties. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor:  http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html)||The primary impacts of the drought during November continued to be agricultural in drought counties. Forage was becoming sparse and the supplemental feeding of hay to livestock continued.  The fire danger threat also increased during November. In addition, reservoir levels continued a gradual fall. Rivers and creeks in the rainfall deficit areas continued a slow fall, and were below baseflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 2 and 4 inches of rain fell in the early morning hours over Laredo causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding was reported on city streets throughout the city of Laredo.  The Laredo Fire Department responded to 29 calls of stranded motorists in high water.  Six homes in the western part of Laredo were flooded, as well as an unknown number of homes around Jacaman Rd.  Water also flooded parts of the Webb County Justice Center and Administration Building in the downtown area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an afternoon of record high temperatures near 80 degrees, an arctic front blasted through the area dropping temperatures down into the 30s by evening. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light icing occurred on some overpasses and roads.  There were a few accidents reported.

Strong high pressure with Canadian origins settled over Deep South Texas overnight on the 2nd and into the 3rd, resulting in prolonged sub freezing conditions in mainly rural areas north of the populated lower Rio Grande Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell below 32 degrees just before midnight, bottoming out in the 20s across most portions of the county by daybreak on the 3rd.  The cooperative observation at Falfurrias, TX, recorded a low of 21 degrees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees and stop signs were blown down at the intersection of FM 2022 and FM 227.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance passing northeastward across east Texas generated strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across the northern Texas Panhandle during the late afternoon and early evening hours producing some hail. No damage or injuries were reported.

An unseasonably deep low pressure system moved across the desert southwest and brought strong gusty winds to far west Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 76 mph was measured in northeast El Paso. At least two car windshields were damaged and one roof was blown off a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dryline within a favorably sheared environment triggered severe thunderstorms in northern Hudspeth County in far west Texas. Penny to quarter size hail fell from these storms.
An upper level disturbance passing northeastward across east Texas generated strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on top of a mobile home located on Fisher Street. Two people were rescued from the home with no reported injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance passing northeastward across east Texas generated strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down in the Highway 75 and the FM 1375 area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance passing northeastward across east Texas generated strong to severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The funnel cloud was sighted between the town of New Waverly and Interstate 45.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was a second round of severe thunderstorms that was caused by the passing of another upper level disturbance. These storms swept through the northern counties of southeast Texas during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were trees downed along Highway 39 in North Zulch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing low level winds preceeding and approaching upper level storm system brought abundant moisture to the area. Despite relatively cool surface temperatures, elevated storms were able to develop. Sufficient  instability allowed for several of these storms to become severe resulting in hail, strong winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred near the Big Spring airport with water approximately ten inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms producing large hail...damaging winds...and a few tornadoes moved across the western and central Texas Panhandle during the afternoon and evening hours. No injuries were reported.

Severe thunderstorms producing large hail...damaging winds...and a few tornadoes moved across the western and central Texas Panhandle during the afternoon and evening hours. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed at 1553 CST about seven miles west southwest of Pringle and moved northeast for about a half mile. The tornado then dissipated at 1554 CST and remained over open country. No damage or injuries were reported. The tornado path length and path width were estimated.

During December, drought conditions persisted across South Texas, and even intensified in some areas. By the end of December, extreme drought conditions existed across Goliad and Victoria counties. Severe drought conditions existed across Live Oak, Bee, Refugio, Aransas, and San Patricio counties. Moderate drought existed across Calhoun, McMullen, La Salle, Nueces, northern Jim Wells and northern Duval counties. Abnormally dry conditions were observed across Webb, Kleberg, southern Jim Wells and southern Duval counties. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor:  http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html)||The primary impacts of the drought during December continued to be agricultural in drought counties. Forage was sparse and the supplemental feeding of hay to livestock continued.  The fire danger threat also increased during the month, especially with the use of fireworks with the approach of New Years. In addition, reservoir levels continued a gradual fall. Rivers and creeks in the rainfall deficit areas continued a slow fall, and were below baseflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow with strong winds of twenty-five to thirty miles an hour with gusts to near forty miles an hour contributed to a rollover of a van on U.S. Highway 287 just south of Cactus in Moore County. Two women were killed in the accident and five other passengers were injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A van traveling north on U.S. 287 when the driver lost control on the icy road and the vehicle skidded into the center median where it rolled over. Two women...whose ages were not known...were killed in the accident and five other passengers were injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved from the southwest United States and into the southern plains during the day. A very unstable airmass was noted across the four state region with abundant low level wind shear and deep layer shear in the mid levels of the atmosphere. A dry line moved into southeast Oklahoma and northeast Texas by late afternoon with scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms developing ahead of the dry line across the region. Thunderstorms produced strong and damaging wind gusts along with large hail across most of the region.

Showers and thunderstorms developed in advance and behind a strong cold front which produced severe thunderstorms earlier the night of February 11th in southeast Oklahoma. As these thunderstorms moved southeast into northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana, they encountered an increasingly unstable atmosphere. The result was widespread large hail across portions of northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana on the morning of February 12th. Damaging wind gusts were also reported across portions of the region as a bow echo developed across extreme northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana ahead of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Hwy 192 near 155 just southwest of Tyler, Texas...a roof was blown off a business.  At Lakeway Harbor on Hwy. 155 near Lake Palestine, a few trees were blown down and a porch was blown off a mobile home.  On Hwy. 346 near Hwy. 69, a resident had six pine trees snapped off and roof damage.  Large tree limbs were also blown down along Hwy. 69.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across portions of northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana during the late evening hours of February 16th.  These storms developed in a very unstable airmass across the Lower Mississippi Valley as an upper level storm system moved out of the southwest United States towards the region.  These storms produced large hail and damaging thunderstorms winds ahead of a strong cold front.  Low level wind shear was also present in the atmosphere and as a result...a tornado developed near the Toledo Bend Reservoir in deep east Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff's office reported a tree down along CR.417 near Hwy. 87 in Shelbyville, Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bell and Milam Counties were considered to be in Severe Drought (D2) or worse as defined by the U.S. Drought Monitor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The western portion of Milam County was undergoing Severe Drought (D2) with Extreme Drought (D3) existing in the extreme western tip of the county.  Oat and wheat products which are now emerging are stressed from the lack of rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bastrop County sheriff's office reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across portions of northeast Texas during the late evening hours of February 15th. These storms developed in a weakly unstable airmass yet they produced large hail due to moderate instability aloft with the approach of an upper level storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of hail was received from the community of Yellow Pine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dry line developed over north central Texas on the afternoon of February 25th and began moving eastward in conjunction with an upper level trough moving through the mid-Mississippi Valley.  Moderate lapse rates and lift associated with the dry line helped spur a line of storms in the late afternoon hours over northeast Texas and southwestern Arkansas.  The storms marched eastward throughout the evening and produced hail mainly under 1 inch in many locations across east Texas, southern Arkansas, and northwest Louisiana with some wind damage reported in southern Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cotulla Fire burned over 19,000 acres near and in the town of Cotulla. (N 28 31.215 W 099 18.708)  The wildfire began on the 31st and was finally contained on the evening of February 2nd.  7 homes were destroyed, almost 30 damaged and nearly 70 threatened.  The fire crossed Interstate 35 from the Northwest to the Southeast of Cotulla.  An additional fire was situated Southeast of Cotulla on Martinez St and Dobie Rd, and threatened propane storage tanks plus numerous surrounding dwellings  Numerous local, state and federal resources responded.

A stationary front which extended across northeast Texas and southwest Arkansas during the afternoon of March 6th separated much colder air across the middle Red River valley of northeast Texas, southeast Oklahoma and southwest Arkansas from a warm and humid airmass across the southern half of northeast Texas as well as northwest and north central Louisiana.  The front began to advance southward during the evening of March 6th allowing the colder airmass north of the front to spill southward during the overnight hours of March 6th and into the predawn hours of March 7th.  A strong upper level storm system moved eastward from the Texas Hill country overnight with rain transitioning into a brief period of sleet followed by moderate to heavy snow at times across the region.  Widespread one to three inches of snow was reported across the northern half of northeast Texas...southeast Oklahoma...southwest Arkansas as well as northwest and north central Louisiana late on March 6th and through the day on March 7th.  Heavier snow amounts were reported across the foothills of the Ouachita Mountains in northern McCurtain County Oklahoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow reported in Tyler, Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along and ahead of an approaching cold front across the middle Red River valley of northeast Texas, southeast Oklahoma as well as southwest Arkansas during the afternoon and evening hours of March 31st. These storms developed in an very unstable atmosphere and produced large hail...damaging wind gusts.  Isolated tornadoes were reported across McCurtain County, Oklahoma late in the evening.  The hail was large enough to break out automobile and home windows across the city of Texarkana, Texas close to midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front during the evening hours of March 13th was located from near southwest Oklahoma onto near southwest Missouri.  A surface dryline extended south from near Oklahoma City to near the interstate 35 corridor across north central Texas.  An upper level disturbance during the late evening and overnight hours transversed across the southern plains which helped to push the front further south and east into the Middle Red River Valley of northeast Texas...southeast Oklahoma and southwest Arkansas.  With the aid of a strong low level jet overnight...a few severe thunderstorms developed across the Red River Valley with large hail being produced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low level winds nearing 60 knots at approximately four thousand feet in the atmosphere mixed down to the surface during the afternoon of March 18th.  These winds, not associated with thunderstorms, resulted in widespread trees and power lines down across portions of northeast Texas as well as northwest Louisiana during the afternoon of the March 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell through a home 5 miles west of Hawkins, Texas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds over a 6 mile stretch and up to 2 miles wide caused major damage in and around the Old Boston, Texas community 5 miles south of New Boston, Texas. Damage was also reported at the Red River Army Depot just east of Old Boston, Texas.

Heavier snow amounts were reported across the foothills of the Ouachita Mountains in northern McCurtain County Oklahoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow was measured in Texarkana, Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms began developing along and behind a cold front as it pushed southward across west Texas. Abundant moisture and marginal instability enhanced this activity and resulted in a few severe thunderstorms that persisted into the early morning hours.
A dryline located just east of the Permian Basin and lower Trans Pecos began retreating westward after sunset. Thunderstorms began to develop and strengthen along this boundary.

Winds in the upper portions of the atmosphere began increasing over the area ahead of an upper level system located near the California/Arizona state line. As this storm system neared the region, increasing mid level winds worked their way down to the surface to produce high winds across the Davis and Guadalupe Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Guadalupe Mountains experienced wind gusts up to 67 mph as high winds affected the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms which began during the evening hours of March 29th, continued into the predawn hours of March 30th.  These thunderstorms continued to develop in an unstable and moist airmass...with large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds the result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell in the Rugby, Texas community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms which began during the evening hours of March 29th, continued into the predawn hours of March 30th.  These thunderstorms continued to develop in an unstable and moist airmass...with large hail and damaging thunderstorm winds the result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northeast winds behind a cold front channelled through Gaudalupe Pass and produced high winds for a couple hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front which extended across northeast Texas and southwest Arkansas during the afternoon of March 6th separated much colder air across the middle Red River valley of northeast Texas, southeast Oklahoma and southwest Arkansas from a warm and humid airmass across the southern half of northeast Texas as well as northwest and north central Louisiana.  The front began to advance southward during the evening of March 6th allowing the colder airmass north of the front to spill southward during the overnight hours of March 6th and into the predawn hours of March 7th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow reported in Pittsburg, Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a cold front, jet streak, and an upper level low pressure are to our north led to a severe weather outbreak across the county warning area.  Thunderstorms formed over Val Verde County around 1215 to 1230 AM. As the storms moved east they intensified quickly. This was associated with a low amplitude mid to high tropospheric trof coming from the west, from 700 to 200 mb. The storms continued east across the hill country to the Austin and Georgetown areas. As a cold front moved south around 730 to 800 AM, the storms intensified over Williamson, Travis and Northern Bastrop Counties.

Severe thunderstorms developed across north Texas and southern Oklahoma during the afternoon of April 3rd and moved east into the region during the evening of April 3rd.  The storms developed in a very unstable airmass with strong low level shear profiles resulting in the storms taking on supercell characteristics.  Large hail, and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts were the result.  The storms continued to train across portions of the region, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines were downed along Highway 82.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season cold front, combined with excellent upper level dynamics, collided with increasing deep layer moisture to produce a prolonged period of locally heavy rains, concentrated across populated areas of Cameron County, during the late afternoon and into the overnight of April 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential thunderstorm rains fell generally between 430 and 7 PM across much of the Brownsville area, then were followed by steadier, locally heavy rains during the mid evening.  Another round of lighter rains developed overnight.  Storm totals ranged from 3 to 5 inches across the area, with the highest totals along a line from Rancho Viejo through Brownsville Airport, extending southeast to the Sabal Palm Audubon Center near the Rio Grande. Minor nuisance flooding was reported in poor drainage areas during the initial torrential rains, but, overall, the rain was welcome relief from drought conditions which had been dominant through most of the spring.

A stationary front which extended across northeast Texas and southwest Arkansas during the afternoon of March 6th separated much colder air across the middle Red River valley of northeast Texas, southeast Oklahoma and southwest Arkansas from a warm and humid airmass across the southern half of northeast Texas as well as northwest and north central Louisiana.  The front began to advance southward during the evening of March 6th allowing the colder airmass north of the front to spill southward during the overnight hours of March 6th and into the predawn hours of March 7th.  A strong upper level storm system moved eastward from the Texas Hill country overnight with rain transitioning into a brief period of sleet followed by moderate to heavy snow at times across the region.  Widespread one to three inches of snow was reported across the northern half of northeast Texas...southeast Oklahoma...southwest Arkansas as well as northwest and north central Louisiana late on March 6th and through the day on March 7th.  Heavier snow amounts were reported across the foothills of the Ouachita Mountains in northern McCurtain County Oklahoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow was reported in Carthage, Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and associated dryline extended just west of southeast Oklahoma and northeast Texas during the afternoon hours of April 25th. Strong heating during the afternoon resulted in a very unstable airmass ahead of the dryline/cold front merger. A negatively tilted upper trough dropped into the plains during the afternoon...which allowed strong thunderstorms to develop along the cold front. Large hail was the primary result across portions of northeast Texas and southeast Oklahoma.

Continuing extreme to exceptional drought across Deep South Texas and the Lower Rio Grande Valley, as well as two dry days on April 28th and 29th (following a late season cold front), and low rainfall from an event on April 27th and 28th, resulted in very low one-hour fuel moisture, which then combined with gusty southeasterly flow and rapid dispersion potential through a dry and elevated mixing layer off the surface to help two wildfires to explosively develop during the afternoon of April 30th. In total, these new fires quickly burned more than 2500 acres, mostly in Brooks County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire, known as the Las Cuatas Fire,located along Farm-to-Market Road (FM) 755 at the intersection with Federal Highway 281 near the|Community of Rachal, quickly grew to 1900 acres, driven by gusty southeast winds and excellent low level dispersion. The fire destroyed two residences, one which was not being lived in, as well as one outbuilding.  Ten other homes were threatened and saved; an estimated 150 persons self-evacuated as the fire neared their homes.||Numerous Volunteer Fire Departments and Emergency Management responders helped in the firefighting effort, from Brooks, Hidalgo, Willacy, and Cameron County. The Texas Department of Public Safety provided aircraft and dozers to fight the blaze. The fire was contained later that night into the morning of May 1st.

Severe thunderstorms carried over from the late night hours of March 31st, into the pre-dawn hours of April 1st across portions of southwest Arkansas along a slow moving cold front.  These thunderstorms produced very large hail across extreme southwest Arkansas as supercells developed and trained across the same general area.  Later that afternoon...as the front continued to push south into Deep East Texas, the front encountered a very unstable environment.  As a result...an isolated severe thunderstorm developed across Angelina County, Texas producing large hail.

Severe thunderstorms developed across north Texas and southern Oklahoma during the afternoon of April 3rd and moved east into the region during the evening of April 3rd.  The storms developed in a very unstable airmass with strong low level shear profiles resulting in the storms taking on supercell characteristics.  Large hail, and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts were the result.  The storms continued to train across portions of the region, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported on the extreme east side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dry line across south central Oklahoma and north central Texas and moved eastward into portions of northeast Texas...southeast Oklahoma...southwest Arkansas and northwest Louisiana.  These thunderstorms developed with the aid of a strong upper level low pressure system that pulled out into the southern and central plains.  These thunderstorms which affected the region during the afternoon...evening and overnight hours of April 8th produced large hail...damaging thunderstorm winds gusts along with very heavy rainfall and an isolated tornado touched down across McCurtain County, Oklahoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed near the community.  Trees were reported down on State Highway 64 where two cars rain into the fallen tree. There were no injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient initially across northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana during the pre dawn hours of April 10th produced non-thunderstorm wind gusts responsible for downing trees and power lines.  Later that morning...a squall line entered northeast Texas and southeast Oklahoma producing mostly wind damage along with some large hail and an isolated tornado in Bowie County Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and powerlines were downed throughout the county.  Damaging wind gusts downed one of the oldest trees in Naples, Texas to fall and cause extensive damage to one of the oldest in town.  Other trees were reported down at the intersection of Hwy 338 and FM 161 along with others in the Omaha area as well.  Wind also leveled the outfield fence at the Pewitt baseball field and uprooted the pole vault pit at the football field.

These thunderstorms which affected the region during the afternoon...evening and overnight hours of April 8th produced large hail...damaging thunderstorm winds gusts along with very heavy rainfall and an isolated tornado touched down across McCurtain County, Oklahoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail smashed through the windshield of a moving vehicle causing a non-life threatening injury to the driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient initially across northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana during the pre dawn hours of April 10th produced non-thunderstorm wind gusts responsible for downing trees and power lines.  Later that morning...a squall line entered northeast Texas and southeast Oklahoma producing mostly wind damage along with some large hail and an isolated tornado in Bowie County Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down across the city.

Severe drought conditions developed across western Webb county. (per the U.S.Drought Monitor: http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html)||The primary impacts of the drought through June continued to be agricultural.  In addition, reservoir levels, while not critically low, did notably decrease during the month of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Hurricane Dolly made landfall across extreme southern Texas around midday on July 23rd, an outer feeder band helped to enhance sea breeze thunderstorm activity across portions of northwest Louisiana and northeast Texas.  These storms were moving from east to west across the region and produced wind speeds of 50 to 60 mph as they decayed.  Isolated reports of damage were received across Harrison County in northeast Texas during the evening hours of July 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were downed on Warren Drive near the Marshall, Texas airport.  Another tree fell on a home near the airport.  No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated and rather weak thunderstorm produced a brief tornado during the afternoon hours over the northeastern Texas Panhandle. No damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief and small rope landspout developed just southwest of Perryton over an open field. No damage or injuries were reported. The path length and path width were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions intensified along and west of Interstate 37 across South Texas during the month of April.  Only 0.43 of rain fell at Laredo, 0.45 at Alice, 1.12 at Robstown, 1.25 at Cotulla, and 1.48 at Kingsville. 2.36 of rain fell at the Corpus Christi International Airport.||By the end of April exceptional drought conditions had developed across Webb county and southwest Duval county. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor: http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html) Extreme drought conditions expanded into La Salle, McMullen, central and northeast Duval, Live Oak, and western Jim Wells counties. Severe drought conditions existed across western Bee and western Kleberg counties. Moderate drought conditions developed once again across western San Patricio, central and western Nueces, and central and eastern Kleberg counties.||The primary impacts of the drought through April continued to be agricultural and fire danger impacts.  Lack of sufficient top soil moisture inhibited row crops from germinating. Soil insects in corn and grain sorghum coupled with high winds through the month caused some stand loss. Lack of soil moisture slowed the growth of cotton and small grains.  Cattle in some areas were being supplemented with hay due to the short grass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the south central Texas Panhandle producing strong gradient winds during the early evening hours. No damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust measured at the Amarillo ASOS which is located six miles east northeast of Amarillo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a cold front, jet streak, and an upper level low pressure are to our north led to a severe weather outbreak across the county warning area.  Thunderstorms formed over Val Verde County around 1215 to 1230 AM. As the storms moved east they intensified quickly. This was associated with a low amplitude mid to high tropospheric trof coming from the west, from 700 to 200 mb. The storms continued east across the hill country to the Austin and Georgetown areas. As a cold front moved south around 730 to 800 AM, the storms intensified over Williamson, Travis and Northern Bastrop Counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of trees down across the northern and eastern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient initially across northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana during the pre dawn hours of April 10th produced non-thunderstorm wind gusts responsible for downing trees and power lines.  Later that morning...a squall line entered northeast Texas and southeast Oklahoma producing mostly wind damage along with some large hail and an isolated tornado in Bowie County Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the tornado damage was north of interstate 30 with some structures showing EF2 damage.  In particular, the cinderblock and brick lawnmower business just north of Hwy 82 was completely destroyed with roofing debris and lawnmower parts thrown to the west and north of the building location.   A brick home several hundred yards from the lawnmower business sustained significant damage to its roof and exterior walls.  A metal shop building built with large metal I-beams was completely destroyed.  I-beams were twisted and thrown in a northerly and westerly direction up to 200 yards from the building location with concrete still attached.  The trees between the large metal building and the interstate were uprooted or snapped in a convergent pattern...indicative of tornadic winds.  In total...12 structures were damaged or destroyed between Hwy 82 and the interstate and numerous trees were downed.  Three tractor trailers were flipped on interstate 30  which resulted in the interstate being shut down and there was one injury.  Further south of Hwy 82 on the Lonestar Army Ammunition Depot, numerous trees were snapped or uprooted and damage to parts of the Depot were reported...although it was not surveyed.  North of interstate 30 along the service road...an outbuilding sales business lost several buildings and had many others damaged.  Along Farm to Market 2253, numerous trees were snapped and uprooted on either side of the road and several sheds and barns were damaged or destroyed.  A greenhouse was severely damaged near the end of the track.  Some homes were also damaged from fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lack of significant rainfall resulted in an expansion of drought conditions across South Texas.  In February only a trace of rainfall fell at Laredo, 0.04 at Cotulla, 0.16 at Corpus Christi and 0.24 at Alice.  By the end of February severe drought conditions had developed across Webb, La Salle and portions of Duval and McMullen counties. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor: http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html)  Moderate drought conditions existed across Live Oak, Jim Wells, Bee, Goliad, Refugio, Aransas, San Patricio, Nueces and Kleberg counties.||The primary impact of the drought through February continued to be a wildfire threat as well as agricultural impacts.

The Cotulla Fire burned over 19,000 acres near and in the town of Cotulla. (N 28 31.215 W 099 18.708) The wildfire began on January 31st and was finally contained on the evening of February 2nd. 7 homes were destroyed, almost 30 damaged and nearly 70 threatened. The fire crossed Interstate 35 from the Northwest to the Southeast of Cotulla. An additional fire was situated Southeast of Cotulla on Martinez St and Dobie Rd, and threatened propane storage tanks plus numerous surrounding dwellings Numerous local, state and federal resources responded.

(N 28 31.215 W 099 18.708) The wildfire began on January 31st and was finally contained on the evening of February 2nd. 7 homes were destroyed, almost 30 damaged and nearly 70 threatened. The fire crossed Interstate 35 from the Northwest to the Southeast of Cotulla. An additional fire was situated Southeast of Cotulla on Martinez St and Dobie Rd, and threatened propane storage tanks plus numerous surrounding dwellings Numerous local, state and federal resources responded.

A wildfire burned approximately three hundred acres about twelve miles northwest of Miami Texas just east of Farm to Market Road 283. The wildfire started about 1445 CST and was contained around 1900 CST. Two homes and four other structures were saved and there were no reports of damage or injuries. The cause of the wildfire was determined to be the result of downed transmission lines.

A stationary front which extended across northeast Texas and southwest Arkansas during the afternoon of March 6th separated much colder air across the middle Red River valley of northeast Texas, southeast Oklahoma and southwest Arkansas from a warm and humid airmass across the southern half of northeast Texas as well as northwest and north central Louisiana.  The front began to advance southward during the evening of March 6th allowing the colder airmass north of the front to spill southward during the overnight hours of March 6th and into the predawn hours of March 7th.  A strong upper level storm system moved eastward from the Texas Hill country overnight with rain transitioning into a brief period of sleet followed by moderate to heavy snow at times across the region.  Widespread one to three inches of snow was reported across the northern half of northeast Texas...southeast Oklahoma...southwest Arkansas as well as northwest and north central Louisiana late on March 6th and through the day on March 7th.  Heavier snow amounts were reported across the foothills of the Ouachita Mountains in northern McCurtain County Oklahoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow was reported in Longview, Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure with Canadian origins settled over Deep South Texas overnight on the 2nd and into the 3rd, resulting in prolonged sub freezing conditions in mainly rural areas north of the populated lower Rio Grande Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell below 32 degrees just before midnight on the 3rd, bottoming out in the lower to mid 20s county-wide by daybreak.  Duration of sub-freezing temperatures from AWOS and Co-operative observing sites was estimated to be 8 to 10 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of Tuesday, June 17th, a Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) developed over western Oklahoma, and surged southeastward into north Texas after sunrise. This storm system produced widespread 60-70 mph wind gusts across much of north Texas, including Dallas, which then spread into east Texas during the early afternoon hours. Numerous trees and power lines were downed across areas generally along and south of Interstate 20, particularly in the city if Tyler, and also Center, Texas. In fact, trees fell atop several buildings in Center, although no injuries were reported. The storms weakened by early evening, as they spread/developed farther southward into southeast Texas and southwest Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a two-story home inside a gated community causing a two-alarm fire in the city of Tyler, Texas.  The home was in the 3500 block of Melanie Court inside the River Oaks community.

An isolated wind gusts just below 70 mph was reported across Bossier Parish in northwest Louisiana during the evening of June 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed near Highway 450 and Highway 80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level shortwave moved eastward across Oklahoma and Arkansas during the late evening and overnight hours of May 7th.  A storm environment consisted of moderate instability but very strong shear present in the lower and mid levels of the atmosphere.  A dry line moved just east of the Interstate 35 corridor of north central Texas before stalling out.  Supercells developed along the dryline across central Texas...moving northeast into northeast Texas and merged with a pre-frontal trough which set up across northeast Texas into southeast Oklahoma and southwest Arkansas.  The result was severe thunderstorms across mainly the southern half of northeast Texas...and much of northwest and north central Louisiana.  Storms contained strong and damaging wind gusts which downed numerous trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed along CR 1277.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system which developed during the early morning hours of May 27th across central Oklahoma moved southeast during the morning and produced widespread wind damage across northeast Texas, southwest Arkansas and northwest Louisiana.  The atmosphere ahead of the complex was very unstable with moisture advection helping to feed the system as it moved southeast out of Oklahoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly winds were sustained at speeds between 25 to 35 mph over much of the west Texas South Plains region during the afternoon of the 4th. An isolated severe gust of 59 mph occurred at the Texas Tech University West Texas Mesonet site near Dimmitt. No damage was reported.
A thunderstorm produced isolated wind damage in Caldwell County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The metal roof on a storage unit was damaged along with some damage to brick walls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm tracked northeastward from the western Permian Basin of west Texas and impacted areas near Brownfield (Terry County). Penny size hail was reported by local emergency management officials southwest of Brownfield along U.S. Highway 385/62.

Isolated thunderstorms impacted the southern Rolling Plains during the evening hours of the 13th. A storm over King County produced severe wind gusts up to 59 mph as measured by the Texas Tech University West Texas Mesonet site southwest of Guthrie. No damage was reported.

An upper level trough over Texas and a speed max rotating through the trough triggered showers and thunderstorms over much of south central Texas.  Flooding rains developed during the predawn hours over Bexar and Atascosa counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey concludes straight line thunderstorm winds produced tree and fence damage near the Live Oak area early in the morning. The area of damage was bounded by Randolph Blvd to the west...Toepperwein Road to the North...Kittyhawk Road to the east and Crestway Drive to the south.  Most of the tree and fence damage was near the intersection of Oconner Road and Randolph.  Winds were estimated at 60 mph.  One inch hail was also observed near the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed under a very unstable airmass across portions of northeast Texas, southwest Arkansas and northwest Louisiana during the morning and afternoon hours of May 2nd. Strong shear in the lower levels of the atmosphere was present during the morning hours such that some thunderstorms produced tornadoes across northeast Texas and southwest Arkansas. Otherwise large hail was the primary severe weather threat with this storm system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dissipating cold front combined with an upper level disturbance and moist flow from the Gulf of Mexico, acted on increasingly unstable air just above the surface to produced several periods of strong thunderstorms from early morning through mid evening of November 13th.  Some of the storms became severe, dropping large hail during the mid morning and evening hours; heavy rainfall adding up to more than 3 inches in portions of Cameron County, with pockets of heavy rain elsewhere in the Lower Rio Grande Valley, produced nuisance minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm with a pronounced hail core rolled through the Donna-Alamo area during the mid morning, dropping large hail in several areas. Nickel size hail fell along the federal highway 83 expressway in Donna, shattering at least one automobile windshield.  Penny size hail fell a bit later in and near Alamo.  In addition to the hail, 2.2 inches of rain fell near the Alamo Country Club in a short period of time, probably less than an hour.||The parent left-moving thunderstorm began across the Rio Grande in northern Mexico a little before 7 AM CST, and moved into extreme southern Hidalgo County near the Santa Ana National Wildlife Refuge around 730 AM. Large hail may well have occurred along this portion of the path, as well as northwest of Donna toward Edinburg, between 830 and 9 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable and moist airmass was in place across southeast Oklahoma, southwest Arkansas, northeast Texas and northern Louisiana the afternoon of May 10th.  A strong upper level trough was present across the central plains and the southern extent of this trough was located across southern Oklahoma and southern Arkansas. A dry line moved eastward across north central Texas and southern Oklahoma during the afternoon but stalled just east of the interstate 35 corridor|of central and north Texas.  A strong cold front eventually caught up with the dry line from the northwest and severe thunderstorms erupted at the dry line, cold front merger across southeastern Oklahoma.  Storms migrated eastward through the night of the 10th with large hail...damaging wind gusts and isolated tornadoes noted across portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed just east of town.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with winds of 95 to 105 mph occurred along CR 241 in Panola County and moved southeastward along CR 224 before lifting. Numerous homes were damaged mainly from falling trees. In particular...a manufactured home had its entire roof peeled and strewn into a field east of the home. A cabin 300 yards east of that home had its entire roof lifted off the wooden frame and thrown into the tree line adjacent to the structure. Numerous large trees both hardwood and softwood were snapped and or uprooted along the path which ended 5 miles southeast along CR 224. Several trees were uprooted or snapped onto homes along CR 241 and CR 248. The tornado was 200 yards wide at its widest point and on the ground until 1203 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough extended from the surface low pressure system south of Wichita Falls, Texas and reached to the northeast through southeast Oklahoma and central Arkansas while a nearly stationary frontal boundary extended from west Texas through the surface low over northwest Texas and into northeast Oklahoma and north Arkansas. Storms developed out ahead of the activity up north into southwest Arkansas and the band between the trough of low pressure and the stationary boundary pushed south.  The storms produced strong wind which downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on a truck along Farm to Market road 195.  No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of Tuesday, June 17th, a Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) developed over western Oklahoma, and surged southeastward into north Texas after sunrise. This storm system produced widespread 60-70 mph wind gusts across much of north Texas, including Dallas, which then spread into east Texas during the early afternoon hours. Numerous trees and power lines were downed across areas generally along and south of Interstate 20, particularly in the city if Tyler, and also Center, Texas. In fact, trees fell atop several buildings in Center, although no injuries were reported. The storms weakened by early evening, as they spread/developed farther southward into southeast Texas and southwest Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of another Mesoscale Convective System (MCS), which developed during the late night hours in Northern Nebraska on Tuesday June 17th, rapidly spread southeastward across the plains states and into southeast Oklahoma, northeast Texas, and northern Louisiana during the day on Wednesday, June 18th. As this disturbance interacted with strong surface heating/instability over east Texas, in vicinity of a weak stationary boundary, which extended from the Louisiana coast northwest into deep east Texas, and into central Texas, isolated severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon/early evening. One severe thunderstorm produced nickel sized hail in the community of Woden, south of Nacogdoches, while a second severe thunderstorm developed along the Nacogdoches/Angelina County line, which later moved south into the city of Lufkin. Multiple reports of quarter to golf ball sized hail were received in Lufkin with this second storm. Small limbs were downed as well from the hail and gusty winds. These storms quickly dissipated after sunset.

The region continued to be under the influence of weak northwest wind flow aloft which allowed for a disturbance to affect the region from the upper levels of the atmosphere.  This combined with high surface dewpoints and a strong low level jet which helped to feed the system...resulting in the development of strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of deep east Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed in town.

July was the third consecutive month with below normal precipitation.  Corn, soybean, cotton, and sorghum production has been significantly impacted by the drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed in a very unstable atmosphere across portions of northeast Texas and extreme northwest Louisiana during the afternoon and evening hours of August 3rd.  Excessive heat with temperatures in the triple digits resulted in strong instability across the region.  A weak disturbance aloft moved westward out of Mississippi and was the trigger for storm initiation.  As a result, severe thunderstorms once again produced strong wind gusts which downed powerlines and large hail was also reported in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Tx. 705 west of Pineland, Texas.

Several episodes of heavy rainfall during the last half of August helped to significantly decrease the drought severity across South Texas.  During the month of August a widespread 4 to 8 inches of rainfall occurred across much of South Texas.||At the beginning of August extreme drought conditions were apparent along and north of a Rockport to Cotulla line, with severe and moderate drought conditions south of this line. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor: http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html)  By the end of August, moderate drought conditions were confined to Victoria, Goliad, Calhoun, Aransas, and Refugio counties.  Abnormally dry conditions were occurring across Bee, eastern San Patricio, and northern La Salle counties.  By the end of August the remaining South Texas counties were no longer considered in drought or abnormally dry conditions.||The primary impacts of the drought through August continued to be agricultural in drought counties across the northeast portions of South Texas.  However, in many areas where rainfall occurred, especially outside of the drought area, cotton harvesting was slowed or halted.  The drops in reservoir levels during July stabilized in August with help from the widespread rainfall.

By the end of August the remaining South Texas counties were no longer considered in drought or abnormally dry conditions. ||The primary impacts of the drought through August continued to be agricultural in drought counties across the northeast portions of South Texas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over northern Potter County and the northwest flow aloft brought the storm southeast through eastern Potter County...western Carson County and then through Armstrong County. This severe thunderstorm with its intense rainfall caused the Prairie Dog Town Fork of the Red River to go above flood stage at the gage site thirteen miles northeast of Wayside. The river rose from its base flow of five and four tenths feet to a crest of ten and four tenths of a foot in forty-five minutes. This is one of the quickest rises recorded at this gage site. No damage or injuries were reported from this river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage at the Prairie Dog Town Fork of the Red River...located thirteen miles northeast of Wayside in southwestern Armstrong County...reached a crest of ten point thirty-seven feet. The flood stage is ten feet. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 1 and 3 inches of rain fell quickly during the early evening hours across western Webb county. Flooding was observed in the city of Laredo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall led to flash flooding of several city streets and creeks in the city of Laredo, most notably in the west-central and northwest portions of the city.  In addition, two teenage boys and an adult male were playing near the flooded Zacate Creek, near Sanchez Street, when they fell into the creek.  The Laredo Fire Department successfully performed a swift water rescue and all three individuals were saved.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rainfall, poor drainage area, and a dry creek led to flash flooding and significant street flooding in and around the Green Acres and Ben Bolt areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed in a very unstable atmosphere across portions of northeast Texas and extreme northwest Louisiana during the afternoon and evening hours of August 3rd.  Excessive heat with temperatures in the triple digits resulted in strong instability across the region.  A weak disturbance aloft moved westward out of Mississippi and was the trigger for storm initiation.  As a result, severe thunderstorms once again produced strong wind gusts which downed powerlines and large hail was also reported in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines were downed in the western portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of heavy rainfall during the last half of August helped to significantly decrease the drought severity across South Texas.  During the month of August a widespread 4 to 8 inches of rainfall occurred across much of South Texas.||At the beginning of August extreme drought conditions were apparent along and north of a Rockport to Cotulla line, with severe and moderate drought conditions south of this line. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor: http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html)  By the end of August, moderate drought conditions were confined to Victoria, Goliad, Calhoun, Aransas, and Refugio counties.  Abnormally dry conditions were occurring across Bee, eastern San Patricio, and northern La Salle counties.  By the end of August the remaining South Texas counties were no longer considered in drought or abnormally dry conditions.||The primary impacts of the drought through August continued to be agricultural in drought counties across the northeast portions of South Texas.  However, in many areas where rainfall occurred, especially outside of the drought area, cotton harvesting was slowed or halted.  The drops in reservoir levels during July stabilized in August with help from the widespread rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Elgin Fire Department reported a funnel cloud near the junction of FM  696 and FM 619 moving toward Lexington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving and weak frontal boundary collided with gulf moisture and approaching shortwave trough over northern Mexico to form convection that developed in the eastern hill country and over the coastal plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The airport manager at the Birds Nest Airport reported hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late afternoon southwest moving thunderstorm produced some damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a power pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture left behind from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lowell was lifted by a weak back door cold front. This was the focus for slow moving thunderstorms with very heavy rain in eastern El Paso County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Horizon Boulevard below Horizon City was impassable to due rapidly moving water and debris. Floodwaters entered at least one home in the Sparks subdivision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Gustav moved through Louisiana and Arkansas causing gusty winds to spread across east Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained wind speeds over 20 MPH with gusts as high as 40 MPH were recorded in Sherman-Denison as T.D. Gustav passed to the east.  Large limbs broke and fell onto power lines, causing power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwestern portions of north Texas were undergoing drought as defined by the U.S. Drought Monitor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The western portion of Bell County was undergoing Severe Drought (D2).  Oat and wheat products which are now emerging are stressed from the lack of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwestern portions of north Texas were undergoing drought as defined by the U.S. Drought Monitor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The western portion of Milam County was undergoing Severe Drought (D2) with the far western tip of the county considered to be in Extreme Drought(D3).  Oat and wheat products which are now emerging are stressed from the lack of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storms and outflow boundaries originating to the north and northeast of our area moved to the southwest and generated some strong winds across portions of southeast Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several large trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike from early afternoon showers and thunderstorms injured a man working outside.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nineteen year old male working outside at a construction site was struck and injured by lightning. The victim was taken to a local hospital and later released having suffered only minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into South Central Texas on September 9 producing scattered rain showers and isolated thunderstorms. Although conditions were not favorable for severe weather, one thunderstorm produced a water spout over Lake Buchanan early in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Llano County sheriff's office and the Lower Colorado River Authority police department reported a water spout over Lake Buchanan in the vicinity of Buchanan Dam. The water spout was short lived and dissipated before reaching land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of moderate to heavy rain with embedded thunderstorms developed over portions of Northeast Texas during the late morning hours of the 9th, ahead of weak cold front and associated series of upper level disturbances translating east across North Texas/Southern Arkansas. The heavier rain bands persisted over the same areas of Smith and Southern Wood counties through early afternoon, with additional rainfall amounts of two to four inches falling over already saturated grounds, where rainfall amounts of two to three inches fell during the evening hours of the 8th. This excessive rainfall resulted in numerous roads being flooded across Smith County. From 8pm on the 8th, through 2 pm on the 9th, 5.84 inches of rain fell at Tyler Pounds Field Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of road closures across the northern and southern portions of Smith County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Colliding outflow boundaries from afternoon showers and thunderstorms produced some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines along FM 111.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During September, drought conditions persisted across northeast portions of the Coastal Bend and Victoria Crossroads. Throughout September, moderate drought conditions persisted across Goliad, Calhoun, Aransas, Refugio, southern Victoria, and northeast Bee counties. Drought conditions intensified into the severe category across northern Victoria county.  Abnormally dry conditions were observed across central Bee and San Patricio counties. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor: http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html) ||The primary impacts of the drought during September continued to be agricultural in drought counties. Reservoir levels at Coleto Creek during September fell slightly.  In addition, rivers and creeks in this rainfall deficit areas continued a slow fall, and were below slightly below baseflow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During September, drought conditions persisted across northeast portions of the Coastal Bend and Victoria Crossroads. Throughout September, moderate drought conditions persisted across Goliad, Calhoun, Aransas, Refugio, southern Victoria, and northeast Bee counties. Drought conditions intensified into the severe category across northern Victoria county. Abnormally dry conditions were observed across central Bee and San Patricio counties. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor: http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html) ||The primary impacts of the drought during September continued to be agricultural in drought counties. Reservoir levels at Coleto Creek during September fell slightly. In addition, rivers and creeks in this rainfall deficit areas continued a slow fall, and were below slightly below baseflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed in a moderately unstable airmass across portions of northeast Texas, southeast Oklahoma and southwest Arkansas.  These thunderstorms developed along a prefrontal surface trough along a corridor of moderate instability and strong deep layer shear aloft.  Some of these storms exhibited rotation as they moved northeast during the late morning and continued into the late evening hours.  Large hail and damaging wind gusts were produced from these thunderstorms across portions of northeast Texas and southwest Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported near Bullard, Texas near Hwy. 69 and FM 2493.

Large hail and damaging wind gusts were produced from these thunderstorms across portions of northeast Texas and southwest Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed in the Huffines and Smyrna communities along FM 1841.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana along a frontal boundary in association with an upper level disturbance.  Enough instability existed in the late afternoon, early evening hours such that a thunderstorms briefly reached severe criteria across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell 3 miles north of Queen City, Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very tight pressure gradient ahead of an approaching cold front resulted in strong west to southwest winds across the southwest Texas Panhandle during the early afternoon hours. No damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured at the Hereford AWOS station located at the Hereford Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very tight pressure gradient ahead of an approaching cold front resulted in strong west to southwest winds across the southwest Texas Panhandle during the early afternoon hours. No damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured at the Hereford AWOS station located at the Hereford Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level shortwave trough and convergence along a surface cold front provided sufficient lift over most of southwest Texas.  Although low level moisture was not too impressive (limiting CAPE values), the 0-6 km bulk shear was near 40-50 kts.  A developing low level jet and strong forcing eventually resulted in a MCS forming over eastern portions of MAF's CWA by 8 PM CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel sized hail falling at the intersection of FM1601 & FM1233.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of northeast Texas and northwest Louisiana along a frontal boundary in association with an upper level disturbance.  Enough instability existed in the late afternoon, early evening hours such that a thunderstorms briefly reached severe criteria across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a salt water disposal tank near mile marker 624 just off Interstate 20 west of Waskom, Texas.  The resulting fire spread to other nearby tanks in the area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Kim Flowers Power Line wildfire began approximately 2022 CST about fourteen miles north northwest of Miami in north central Roberts County...or just east of Farm Market Road 283 southeast of the intersection with Texas Highway 70. Approximately four hundred and ten acres were burned. Although no structural damage and no injuries were reported...one home was saved. The cause of the wildfire was determined to be from power lines. A total of eight fire departments responded to the wildfire which was contained by 2400 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds of fifteen to twenty-five knots and one to two inches of snow contributed to at least seventy-five accidents in the southern half of the Texas Panhandle. The winds and snow resulted in near whiteout conditions and very treacherous driving conditions. One woman was killed in an accident in northwest Amarillo and several other people were injured in a separate vehicle accident on Interstate 40 in southeastern Oldham County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds and one to one and a half inches of snow combined to cause treacherous driving conditions and near zero mile visibilities. One woman was killed when her vehicle spun out for about one hundred yards on the icy road in northwest Amarillo on Soncy Road (Loop 335) near Wildcat Bluff and collided with a truck carrying jet fuel.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds along with one to two inches of snow combined to cause near whiteout conditions and very treacherous driving conditions. A twenty vehicle accident on eastbound Interstate 40 near Wildorado resulted in at least seven people injured...two of whom were in critical condition.
