EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of heavy rainfall during the last half of August helped to significantly decrease the drought severity across South Texas.  During the month of August a widespread 4 to 8 inches of rainfall occurred across much of South Texas.||At the beginning of August extreme drought conditions were apparent along and north of a Rockport to Cotulla line, with severe and moderate drought conditions south of this line. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor: http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html)  By the end of August, moderate drought conditions were confined to Victoria, Goliad, Calhoun, Aransas, and Refugio counties.  Abnormally dry conditions were occurring across Bee, eastern San Patricio, and northern La Salle counties.  By the end of August the remaining South Texas counties were no longer considered in drought or abnormally dry conditions.||The primary impacts of the drought through August continued to be agricultural in drought counties across the northeast portions of South Texas.  However, in many areas where rainfall occurred, especially outside of the drought area, cotton harvesting was slowed or halted.  The drops in reservoir levels during July stabilized in August with help from the widespread rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several episodes of heavy rainfall during the last half of August helped to significantly decrease the drought severity across South Texas. During the month of August a widespread 4 to 8 inches of rainfall occurred across much of South Texas. ||At the beginning of August extreme drought conditions were apparent along and north of a Rockport to Cotulla line, with severe and moderate drought conditions south of this line. (per the U.S. Drought Monitor: http://www.drought.unl.edu/dm/monitor.html) By the end of August, moderate drought conditions were confined to Victoria, Goliad, Calhoun, Aransas, and Refugio counties. Abnormally dry conditions were occurring across Bee, eastern San Patricio, and northern La Salle counties. By the end of August the remaining South Texas counties were no longer considered in drought or abnormally dry conditions. ||The primary impacts of the drought through August continued to be agricultural in drought counties across the northeast portions of South Texas. However, in many areas where rainfall occurred, especially outside of the drought area, cotton harvesting was slowed or halted. The drops in reservoir levels during July stabilized in August with help from the widespread rainfall.

A dissipating cold front combined with an upper level disturbance and moist flow from the Gulf of Mexico, acted on increasingly unstable air just above the surface to produced several periods of strong thunderstorms from early morning through mid evening of November 13th.  Some of the storms became severe, dropping large hail during the mid morning and evening hours; heavy rainfall adding up to more than 3 inches in portions of Cameron County, with pockets of heavy rain elsewhere in the Lower Rio Grande Valley, produced nuisance minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy rains accompanied several thunderstorms that affected the Brownsville-Port Isabel and Laguna Vista area during much of the day on the 13th.  The heaviest rains, dropping more than in inch in an hour, occurred generally between 9 and 1045 AM drifting northeast from the southeast portion of Brownsville toward Port Isabel and Laguna Vista.  ||Typical poor drainage flooding was likely observed on portions of Boca Chica Blvd. mainly east of the highway 77 expressway in Brownsville. ||Calendar day rain totals at Brownsville/International Airport and Cameron County Airport (Bayview) were 2.76 and 3.05 inches, respectively.

However, in many areas where rainfall occurred, especially outside of the drought area, cotton harvesting was slowed or halted. The drops in reservoir levels during July stabilized in August with help from the widespread rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly developing storm cell, likely aided by convergence from outflow boundaries of earlier cells in the nearshore waters east of South Padre Island, and a developing Gulf Coast sea breeze, produced a quick-hitting waterspout which moved ashore in Laguna Heights before dissipating at the south edge of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved from the Laguna Madre through the small town of Laguna Heights shortly after noon on November 7th, knocking out power to a very small part of town, including at Bayside Marine at the intersection of Roy St. and SR 100, where witnesses saw the funnel knock down power lines and eventually pick up debris in fields just west of town before dissipating.  Peak winds were estimated to be around 55 mph.||A survey indicated a few medium to large limbs blown down, as well as turned up aluminum facia on two older, trailer-style mobile homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level low pressure system moved across Southeast Texas late on December 10, 2008 into the morning hours of December 11, 2008.  As cold air aloft associated with this system interacted with widespread rainfall ongoing over the area, precipitation began mixing with sleet and snow, and eventually changed over to all snow in many locations.  This rare snow event lasted from 5 to 7 hours, with numerous reports of large snowflakes to the size of half dollars, along with a few reports of thundersnow.  Snow totals ranged from a trace or less across far northern areas of southeast Texas and along the coast, to over 5 inches in western Hardin and Jefferson counties.||Snow totals across southeast Texas included 3 inches in Woodville, 3 inches in Warren, 0.5 inches in Jasper, 2.2 inches in Wildwood, 2 inches in Silsbee, 4 inches in Lumberton, 4 inches in Meeker, 2 inches in downtown Beaumont, 3 inches in Nederland, 1 inch in Port Arthur, 2 inches in Vidor, and 1 inch in Orange.||For Beaumont/Port Arthur, this was the earliest measurable snowfall on record for the fall/winter season.  The previous earliest snowfall was on December 22, 1989.  This snow event also set the all-time record snowfall total for the month of December, with 1.8 inches recorded.  The previous record was 0.7 inches on December 22, 1989.  Finally, this was only the second time in history measurable snow has occurred in the month of December.

Warm moist air overran a layer of sub-freezing air at the surface, causing freezing drizzle to spread across north Texas.  Dozens of wrecks were reported Tuesday as the freezing drizzle coated roads and bridges and temperatures remained below freezing throughout the day.  There were numerous injuries and several fatalities throughout the event.  Indirect damages are estimated at $750,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle slipped on ice on Highway 121 in Haltom City and slammed into the guardrails, killing the driver.  Portions of I-20 was closed due to ice.  Numerous other accidents occurred on I-30 and I-35 due to icy conditions on the roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper air storm system translated over the Southern Plains on the 14th and resulted in widespread high winds and blowing dust over the western South Plains of west Texas. Severe wind gusts were initially recorded by the Texas Tech University West Texas Mesonet shortly before 11:00 CST in Yoakum and Terry Counties. During the next three hours, dust and damaging wind gusts spread northeastward over the Lubbock area (Lubbock County). Minor roof damage to a few structures was reported, including a newly constructed church in southwest Lubbock which had a portion of its metal roof peeled off in the winds. No injuries were reported.

An arctic cold front pushed southward over the west Texas South Plains during the evening hours of the 8th, and light snow began to fall over much of the South Plains region overnight and into the morning hours of the 9th. Although no snow accumulations occurred, the onset of freezing temperatures and light wintry precipitation contributed to at least one vehicular accident in Lubbock. A motorist lost control of his pickup truck on west Loop 289 near 23rd Street around midnight CST, and was treated for minor injuries.

Strong low pressure north of the region resulted in a dryline separating warm most air from much drier air across Oklahoma. A cold front caught up to the dryline during the morning hours of January 8th with strong to severe thunderstorms forming on the dryline. The line of thunderstorms originated across northeast Texas and southeast Oklahoma and progressed eastward into southwest Arkansas. Severe thunderstorms produced mostly large hail but isolated strong winds were also reported within the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported at a convenience store.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance brought 1 to 2 inches of snow to Southeast Texas, but with reports of 3 to 5 inches in some places. Some of the maximum amounts include 5 inches at Winnie and Liberty, and 3 to 4 inches at Anahuac, Baytown and LaPorte. The snowfall tied or set records for the earliest accumulating snow at Houston, College Station and Galveston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches reported near Madisonville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A crane collapsed at Lewisville Lake after winds caused a barge to break loose from its chains and crash into rocks.  Sustained winds were between 20 and 25 MPH throughout the day with gusts to 35 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very tight pressure gradient developed behind a strong cold front across much of the region in the wake of a departing upper trough of low pressure. Southerly winds of 15 to 25 mph were observed out ahead of the cold front the morning of the 29th, but quickly veered west northwest areawide by afternoon in wake of the frontal passage. This resulted in sustained wind speeds increasing to 25 to 35 mph, with gusts to 50 to 55 mph during the mid and late afternoon, especially over southeast Oklahoma, extreme northeast Texas, southwest Arkansas, and north Louisiana. Widespread reports of downed trees and power lines were received across the region, with at least 31000 residents without power during the late afternoon and evening of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed through much of the county.  Bowie County 911 recorded over 150 calls of various damage all throughout the county.  A peak wind gusts of 53 mph was measured at the Texarkana Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm moist air overran a layer of sub-freezing air at the surface, causing freezing drizzle to spread across north Texas.  Dozens of wrecks were reported Tuesday as the freezing drizzle coated roads and bridges and temperatures remained below freezing throughout the day.  There were numerous injuries and several fatalities throughout the event.  Indirect damages are estimated at $750,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than two-dozen accidents were reported around the county due to icy conditions.  There were several minor injuries and four major injuries.  Highway 24/50 and Highway 11 were closed for several hours after a string of accidents occurred.  A man was injured after jumping off an interstate bridge north of Commerce to avoid sliding vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained severe to extreme over portions of Bell and Milam Counties according to the U.S. Drought Monitor.  Vegetation and livestock continued to suffer consequences of the lack of rain.  One half of the winter wheat crop was in poor or very poor condition, while 75% of the winter oat crop was rated poor or very poor.  Grazing land was also considered to be in poor condition, which has affected the cattle industry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About two-thirds of the county was considered to be in severe to extreme drought according to the U.S. Drought Monitor.  Vegetation and livestock continued to suffer consequences of the lack of rain.  One half of the winter wheat crop was in poor or very poor condition, while 75% of the winter oat crop was rated poor or very poor.  Grazing land was also considered to be in poor condition, which has affected the cattle industry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance brought 1 to 2 inches of snow to Southeast Texas, but with reports of 3 to 5 inches in some places. Some of the maximum amounts include 5 inches at Winnie and Liberty, and 3 to 4 inches at Anahuac, Baytown and LaPorte. The snowfall tied or set records for the earliest accumulating snow at Houston, College Station and Galveston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One and a half to two inches reported near College Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Polaris Fire, which began on private land as a result of an all terrain vehicle, spread into the Padre Island National Seashore in Kleberg County. (N27 32.42 W97 15.8)  The fire burned approximately 5,000 acres.  No homes or structures were threatened.   Numerous local, state, federal and private resources responded. The exact beginning time was not known.

The Griffen-Sorenson Fire occurred in rural San Patricio County, and was caused by an electrical line spark and wind shift. (N27 53.7910 W097 33.9287) The fire began on 12/11/08 and was finally contained on 12/13/08, after burning approximately 1,500 acres.  No homes or structures were threatened, however smoke did affect portions of Corpus Christi and Calallen. Several local, state, and private resources responded. The exact beginning time was not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of storms moved across north Texas on April 8th through 10th.  Numerous reports of large hail were received on Tuesday evening.  A lone supercell evolved during the afternoon hours of the 9th, producing tornadoes and large hail along its path.  Later that evening and into Thursday morning a squall line tracked across north Texas, producing damaging winds and tornadoes into the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Fort Worth found that a tornado touched down near Happy Hill and traveled northeast approximately three miles to Pleasant Point where it dissipated.  The tornado caused significant roof damage to several homes.  Three homes were considered total losses.  At least thirty other homes received minor to major damage.  Many sheds and outbuildings were damaged or destroyed.  Several trees were uprooted and at least two of them fell onto houses.  Power lines were also downed along the track.  This tornado was rated an EF-1 with maximum wind speeds between 90 and 95 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The eye of Hurricane Ike moved ashore in Galveston County near the city of Galveston. At landfall, Ike had a central pressure of 951.6 mb, as measured at Galveston Pleasure Pier, and a maximum estimated storm surge of 17 feet over portions of Chambers County and the Bolivar Peninsula. Maximum sustained winds at landfall were estimated at 95 knots (110 mph) with gusts to 110 knots (127 mph). A ship near the coast recorded a wind gust of 105 knots as the eye came through. At landfall, Ike was a Category 2 hurricane on the Saffir-Simpson scale based on wind speed, but due to its large size, had a storm surge more typical of a category 3 or 4. The height of the storm tide ranged from 4 to 6 feet in Matagorda county, 6 to 9 feet in Brazoria county, 10 to 13 feet along most of Galveston Island and Galveston Bay, to as high as 17 feet over portions of the Bolivar Peninsula and Chambers County. The majority of property damage at the coast was a result of storm tide. ||Collectively, damage amounts are estimated to be near 14 billion dollars over the counties of Harris, Chambers, Galveston, Liberty, Polk, Matagorda, Brazoria, Fort Bend, San Jacinto, and Montgomery with an estimated 8 billion of that due to storm surge in coastal Galveston, Harris and Chambers Counties. Fresh water flooding also occurred near the city of Houston where up to 14 inches of rain fell over a two day period, first from Ike, then from a line of thunderstorms associated with a cold front moving through the following day. The number of fatalities directly related to Ike was 12 in the aforementioned counties with 11 of those occurring in Galveston County from drowning due to the storm surge. In addition, there were at least 25 fatalities indirectly related to Ike, either due to carbon monoxide poisoning from generators, accidents while clearing debris, or house fires from candles. There were no known tornadoes associated with Ike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ike produced damage due to high storm surge and high winds along Galveston Island, the Bolivar Peninsula, and along the Galveston Bay. Storm tides of 10 to 15 feet above mean sea level were observed in these areas. Several indirect fatalities due to preexisting medical conditions, lack of power and availability to dialysis, etc. Number of injuries unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southward-moving severe thunderstorms developed in the early afternoon and produced hail and wind damage in their path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds collapsed the top floor of a building under construction in Rice Village near Sunset. The was one fatality and seven injuries in the collapse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level shortwave troughs moved across the region, affecting portions of southwest Texas during the afternoon and evening hours.  Southwest to westerly winds, associated with the mid-level jet max in the base of the trough, increased across much of southeast New Mexico, the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains and portions of the northern Permian Basin.

Lift over a cold layer of air at the surface resulted in a glazing of light freezing rain.  Even though the layer of ice was thin, numerous wrecks resulted with a few injuries.  Indirect damages are estimated at $150,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five weather-related accidents occurred on icy streets and bridges across the county.  There were no injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service in Fort Worth found that a tornado touched down in DeSoto along Meadowbrook Road just west of Hampton Road.  The tornado traveled east-northeast to the eastern edge of the city just north of Wintergreen Road.  Several homes suffered roof damage with one home losing the majority of its roof.  There were around twenty homes that sustained damage.  Many downed trees, power poles, and power lines were noted along with damage to sheds and outbuildings. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure north of the region resulted in a dryline separating warm most air from much drier air across Oklahoma. A cold front caught up to the dryline during the morning hours of January 8th with strong to severe thunderstorms forming on the dryline. The line of thunderstorms originated across northeast Texas and southeast Oklahoma and progressed eastward into southwest Arkansas. Severe thunderstorms produced mostly large hail but isolated strong winds were also reported within the thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northeast Texas during the day Saturday, December 27th.  Near record high temps with dewpoints in the 60s ahead of the front along with a strong low level jet of 50 knots made for an environment favorable for tornadoes.  Scattered strong thunderstorms developed along the cold front across northeast Texas.  A tornado was reported across Franklin County, Texas but the majority of the wind damage across the rest of northeast Texas was non-convective in nature as a result of strong post-frontal wind gusts.  The strong non-thunderstorm winds resulted in a few downed trees and large tree limbs...mainly across Smith, Bowie, Cherokee Counties.  Some power was knocked out in a few locations with some roof damage reported west of Tyler, Texas in Smith County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado with winds of approximately 65 mph occurred on the east side of CR 4315 near a cemetary.  Several large trees and branches were downed, one on a home.  A large metal roof was also peeled from a barn|and insulation was thrown into nearby trees.

A cold upper level low pressure system moved across Southeast Texas late on December 10, 2008 into the morning hours of December 11, 2008.  As cold air aloft associated with this system interacted with widespread rainfall ongoing over the area, precipitation began mixing with sleet and snow, and eventually changed over to all snow in many locations.  This rare snow event lasted from 5 to 7 hours, with numerous reports of large snowflakes to the size of half dollars, along with a few reports of thundersnow.  Snow totals ranged from a trace or less across far northern areas of southeast Texas and along the coast, to over 5 inches in western Hardin and Jefferson counties.||Snow totals across southeast Texas included 3 inches in Woodville, 3 inches in Warren, 0.5 inches in Jasper, 2.2 inches in Wildwood, 2 inches in Silsbee, 4 inches in Lumberton, 4 inches in Meeker, 2 inches in downtown Beaumont, 3 inches in Nederland, 1 inch in Port Arthur, 2 inches in Vidor, and 1 inch in Orange.||For Beaumont/Port Arthur, this was the earliest measurable snowfall on record for the fall/winter season.  The previous earliest snowfall was on December 22, 1989.  This snow event also set the all-time record snowfall total for the month of December, with 1.8 inches recorded.  The previous record was 0.7 inches on December 22, 1989.  Finally, this was only the second time in history measurable snow has occurred in the month of December.

A dry line moved across north Texas as an upper level trough moved through the Desert Southwest.  A line of thunderstorms developed and became severe as the night wore on.  Two tornadoes and several downbursts caused significant damage across Grayson County.  Strong winds followed behind a cold front and caused damage to power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To the east of the tornado, thunderstorm winds damaged roofs and storage buildings east of Collinsville.

Although only light accumulations were reported at a few locations, visibilities were significantly restricted during the morning rush hour, and temperatures in the lower 20s caused the snow to quickly freeze onto area roadways. ||Despite the fact that the storm's impact was brief, only two hours in duration, it contributed to more than 90 traffic accidents across the region and at least seven minor injuries. A total of 83 vehicular accidents were reported in Lubbock (Lubbock County), where all of the reported injuries occurred. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ben Bolt Fire burned around 60 acres, however it threatened the town of Ben Bolt, forcing evacuations.  (N27 38 8.49, W98 04 9.3)  500 homes were threatened and saved.  Several homes did receive minor damage.  One middle school and 271 structures were evacuated.  A fiber optic line was destoyed in the fire, causing a complete phone outage for the cities of Falfurrias, Premont and Ben Bolt.  The Alice FD, Alice EMC, Jim Wells Co EMC, Jim Wells Co Judge, Texas Forest Service, DPS and Red Cross responded.  The fire continued into the early morning hours of February 1st.

A strong upper level storm system moved from the southwest United States and into the southern plains during the day. A very unstable airmass was noted across the four state region with abundant low level wind shear and deep layer shear in the mid levels of the atmosphere. A dry line moved into southeast Oklahoma and northeast Texas by late afternoon with scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms developing ahead of the dry line across the region. Thunderstorms produced strong and damaging wind gusts along with large hail across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage was widespread in Bullard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough of low pressure along the lee of the Rockies deepened and resulted in strong west and southwest winds across much of the Texas Panhandle. The roof was blown off of a grocery store...which knocked down power lines and caused damage to cars in the nearby parking lot.

Windy and very dry conditions followed a cold front during the afternoon of the 31st.  Sustained northwest winds 20 to 25 mph, gusting up to 35 mph in some locations, combining with relative humidity dropping to 10 percent or lower, increased the likelihood of rapid wild fire spread.  At least two notable fires developed and rapidly spread during the afternoon.  Despite welcome rains during the middle of the month, the western portion of the lower Rio Grande Valley remained in moderate drought at month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grass wildfire covering over 300 acres occurred January 31, 2008 near La Gloria Texas in Starr County. The fire was reported by the United States Fish and Wildlife Service.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of strong west winds, very dry air and dry vegetation resulted in critical to extreme fire conditions across West Central Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire of unknown size destroyed one outbuilding in Maryneal. One fire truck was destroyed due to a collision with a truck tractor semi-trailer, caused by low visibilities from dense smoke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic Front and a weak upper level disturbance combined to produce areas of light freezing rain across the Big Country during the morning hours of the 22nd.  The freezing rain caused ice to form on area roads and bridges and resulted in several accidents along the I20 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Abilene Police Department reported a total of 30 accidents had occurred as a result of the light freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly winds brought very dry conditions to much of the western South Plains and the extreme southwestern Texas Panhandle during the afternoon hours of the 28th. A 20,000 acre wildfire ignited within this environment south-southwest of Muleshoe (Bailey County) and quickly spread northeastward threatening the town. The fire burned out of control for nearly five hours before it was temporarily contained. Two firefighters were treated for smoke inhalation. ||During the pre-dawn hours of the 29th, a passing cold front re-intensified winds. The winds fanned lingering hotspots at the wildfire burn site, and resulted in a re-surgance of the fire. A mobile home and a few vehicles were destroyed. The occupant of the mobile home suffered serious injuries. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind-driven wildfire ignited three miles south-southwest of Muleshoe shortly before 15:30 CST and rapidly spread northeastward. The wildfire burned an estimated 20,000 acres of brush and grassland atop the area's sand hills. The city of Muleshoe was briefly threatened by the blaze and some homes were evacuated, but the fire ran just southeast of the town. However, a few outbuildings and at least one barn were destroyed. Two firefighters also were treated for smoke inhalation. In addition, a number of roadways were closed by local officials due to the fire and associated smoke, including U.S. Highway 84. ||A resurgence of strong winds around 04:00 CST caused a flare up of the fire east of Muleshoe. A mobile home and a few vehicles were destroyed. The occupant of the mobile home suffered third-degree burn injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire consumed 40,000 to 50,000 bales of cotton bur west of Floydada. Strong westerly winds spread the fire through rangeland and flames threatened a home. Seven firefighting agencies battled the blaze through the daytime hours of the 29th, and the fire was contained to the cotton burs by mid-evening by fire-breaks. The burs continued to smolder for a number of days after the fire. The estimated value of the destroyed cotton burs was $2 million.

The passage of a Canadian cold front and a vigorous storm system combined to produce a brief bout of dangerous winter weather over much of the South Plains region of west Texas early on the 31st.||Northwesterly winds sustained at speeds in excess of 30 mph combined with light to moderate snowfall to result in widespread blowing snow. Although only light accumulations were reported at a few locations, visibilities were significantly restricted during the morning rush hour, and temperatures in the lower 20s caused the snow to quickly freeze onto area roadways. ||Despite the fact that the storm's impact was brief, only two hours in duration, it contributed to more than 90 traffic accidents across the region and at least seven minor injuries. A total of 83 vehicular accidents were reported in Lubbock (Lubbock County), where all of the reported injuries occurred. Weather related collisions also were reported in Plainview, including one which involved a school bus. Total damages were estimated to be $900,000.||Most locations recorded less than one inch of total snow accumulation, however, a list of South Plains cities reporting one inch or greater accumulations included: 2 inches at Silverton (Briscoe County) with drifts up to 6 inches deep, 1 inch Bovina (Parmer County), 1 inch at Dimmitt (Castro County), 1 inch at Muleshoe (Bailey County), 1 inch at Plainview (Hale County), and 1 inch at Tulia (Swisher County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The porch roof peeled off a mobile home which then caused the main roof of the home to peel back. The home was located on Baker Lane just outside of Leander. There was also a large grass fire which started just after noon near FM 1431 and Sam Bass Road causing the closure of eastbound FM 1431 at Raley Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the Anson Fire Department reported several accidents in and around Anson as a result of the light freezing rain.

Two to three inches of snow and windy conditions created areas of near zero visibilities in blowing snow...which resulted in a forty-two car accident on Interstate 40. The accident occurred in the eastbound lanes just west of Whitaker Road at 0542 CST. One person was killed and two people were injured. A sixty-five year-old Amarillo man was identified as the lone fatality. A fire truck from the Amarillo Fire Department became involved in the accident on Interstate 40 when it rolled over while approaching the accident and landed upside down. No one from the four member crew of the fire truck were injured. A thirty-seven year-old man was also killed in a separate accident in the three hundred block of East St. Francis Avenue at 0730 CST due to the poor weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thirty-seven year old man was killed in the morning hours in a vehicle accident which occurred at 0730 CST in the three hundred block of East St. Francis Avenue. The victim was killed when the vehicle he was driving was hit by a pickup truck. Two to three inches of snow along with blowing snow with winds of thirty-five miles per hour contributed to the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large wildfire started five miles north of Pantex around 1400 CST and burned approximately one thousand acres. An old barn was destroyed...yet no injuries were reported. The wildfire was brought under control around 1430 CST near County Road G and County Road 15. The fire line was four miles long and a half mile wide. The cause of the wildfire was a faulty lightning arrestor.

A large wildfire started about four miles south southeast of Sanford around 1400 CST at Windsong Road and Texas State Highway 136. The wildfire moved at a speed of thirty-five miles per hour and burned two and a half miles of open range...or about three hundred acres. The wildfire was contained before reaching the Meadowlark Hill housing division near Borger Texas...however people were asked to evacuate their homes for a brief period. Some fencing was damaged. Texas Highway 136 was partially closed near Meadowlark Hill with only two lanes of traffic open. The wildfire was reported contained around 1600 CST. Four firefighters were injured...including one person who was treated for smoke inhalation.

Strong winds in the mid levels of the atmosphere, well in advance of an upper level system located along the Pacific Coast, resulted in high winds across portions of the Guadalupe Mountains during the morning and afternoon hours of Saturday, January 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were reported across the Guadalupe Mountains, Saturday morning and afternoon.  A 66 mph peak wind was reported at the Guadalupe Bowl during the mid morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large wildfire began about two miles east of Fritch around 1400 CST on Farm to Market Road 687 about one mile north of Texas State Highway 136. The wildfire spread to the east and northeast and ended up close to Farm to Market Road 1319. Approximately two thousand eight hundred acres were burned with more than seven fire department units responding to the wildfire. The wildfire was reported to be under control around 1730 CST. The cause of the wildfire was determined to be arson. Twenty-six people were evacuated. One firefighter received third-degree burns to his hand. Four others suffered minor injuries. The wildfire threatened several housing developments...but no structures were damaged. Two Borger fire trucks were damaged and had to be towed after colliding while fighting the wildfire. The two trucks collided because of low visibility from smoke.

Two to three inches of snow and windy conditions created areas of near zero visibilities in blowing snow...which resulted in a forty-two car accident on Interstate 40. The accident occurred in the eastbound lanes just west of Whitaker Road at 0542 CST. One person was killed and two people were injured. A sixty-five year-old Amarillo man was identified as the lone fatality. A fire truck from the Amarillo Fire Department became involved in the accident on Interstate 40 when it rolled over while approaching the accident and landed upside down. No one from the four member crew of the fire truck were injured. A thirty-seven year-old man was also killed in a separate accident in the three hundred block of East St. Francis Avenue at 0730 CST due to the poor weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow...between two and three inches...and blowing snow with winds of thirty-five miles per hour...resulted in a forty-two car accident on Interstate 40 in the eastbound lanes of the sixty-seven hundred block just west of Whitaker Road at 0542 CST. One person was killed and two people were injured. A sixty-five year old Amarillo man was identified as the victim in the accident. A fire truck from the Amarillo Fire Department became involved in the accident on Interstate 40 when it rolled over while approaching the accident and laid upside down on its top. No one from the four member crew of the fire truck were injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large wildfire started around 1500 CST about one and a half miles north of Stinnett in Hutchinson County...just east of State Highway 136 and 207...and just north of County Road O. The wildfire burned approximately one thousand three hundred acres. Although no damage or injuries were reported...one home and two other structures were saved. The wildfire was contained around 0000 CST. Nine fire departments along with the Texas Forest Service responded to this wildfire.

Strong winds in the mid levels of the atmosphere, well in advance of an upper level system located along the Pacific Coast, resulted in high winds across portions of the Guadalupe Mountains during the morning and afternoon hours of Saturday, January 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds increased during the overnight hours, early Sunday morning.  A peak wind of 59 mph was measured at Guadalupe Pass around 2 am MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation developed over parts of northern Texas before sunrise on the 3rd. This precipitation moved over surface temperatures in the upper 20s and lower 30s, which caused it to freeze on contact. Precipitation coverage was not widespread, but enough ice accumulated on roadways that caused numerous traffic accidents. Monetary damages were estimated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately a 30 minute period of light sleet and freezing rain with 1/16 inches of glaze on area roads. Numerous accidents were reported. Monetary damages were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large wildfire began around 1300 CST approximately seven and a half miles southeast of McLean in northwestern Wheeler County...or about a half mile northwest of the intersection of Farm to Market Road 3143 and County Road 30. The wildfire burned four hundred acres. No damage or injuries were reported...however three homes were saved. Five fire departments responded to this wildfire. The fire was contained around 1600 CST. Welding equipment use was determined to be the cause of the wildfire.

A large wildfire began about two and a half miles north of Allison...or near the intersection of Texas State Highway 277 and Texas State Highway 2124 around 1300 CST. The wildfire burned approximately three hundred and twenty-five acres...however no damage or injuries were reported. Ten fire departments responded to the wildfire...which was contained around 1800 CST.

A surface trough of low pressure along the lee of the Rockies deepened and resulted in strong west and southwest winds across much of the Texas Panhandle. The strong winds caused damage in Booker...in the extreme northeast Texas Panhandle. The roof was blown off of a grocery store...which knocked down power lines and caused damage to cars in the nearby parking lot.

Strong high pressure with Canadian origins settled over Deep South Texas overnight on the 2nd and into the 3rd, resulting in prolonged sub freezing conditions in mainly rural areas north of the populated lower Rio Grande Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell below freezing between 2 and 4 AM, and bottomed out generally between 25 and 30 degrees by daybreak.  Duration of subfreezing temperatures in rural Starr, northern Hidalgo, and southeastern Zapata county ranged from 5 to 7 hours.

There were a couple reports of damage as a cold front moved through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds behind a cold front ripped a sheet metal roof off of a cabinet shop on Main Street in Deport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were a couple reports of damage as a cold front moved through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house on CR 420, causing heavy damage.  Apparently the lightning struck a television antenna on the left side of the house and followed copper wiring inside the house.  The house had recently been sold and was listed at $289,000.  Two firefighters were treated for minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was one report of wind damage as a cold front approached the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds ahead of an approaching cold front caused a large tree to fall over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were dozens of reports of damage due to strong winds as a powerful low pressure system and cold front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Signs were blown down in McKinney, trees were blown over and snapped in Melissa, and an RV was blown over in Anna.  Numerous other lighweight objects such as lawn furniture, trampolines, and metal roofs were blown around.  One heavy brick column was broken at the entrance to a subdivision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a central Texas dry line boundary as it moved into the southeastern portion of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail at State Highway 21 West and FM 908.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a central Texas dry line boundary as it moved into the southeastern portion of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail reported in the town of Iola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a central Texas dry line boundary as it moved into the southeastern portion of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail reported at the Office of Emergency Management in Navasota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through South Central Texas on January 31, 2008 causing strong, gusty winds during the day. Several locations incurred damage from these winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of the roof were blown off of the University of Texas Health Science Center School of Nursing on Floyd Curl Drive in San Antonio. In addition, there were several grass fires in the county one of which caused firefighters to evacuate some elderly people in San Antonio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were dozens of reports of damage due to strong winds as a powerful low pressure system and cold front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fires fueled by sustained winds around 40 MPH fanned a fire near Reno.  A business housed in a mobile home sustained about $100,000 worth of damage.  A pickup truck, hundreds of tires, and various other equipment were destroyed.  A total of 18 structures were lost with nearly 3,500 acres burned.  Hundreds of acres of hay were also lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved from the southwest United States and into the southern plains during the day. A very unstable airmass was noted across the four state region with abundant low level wind shear and deep layer shear in the mid levels of the atmosphere. A dry line moved into southeast Oklahoma and northeast Texas by late afternoon with scattered to numerous severe thunderstorms developing ahead of the dry line across the region. Thunderstorms produced strong and damaging wind gusts along with large hail across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and powerlines were downed all across the county. The worst damage was reported across the western portions of the county including several trees which were downed along Hwy. 42, north of New London, Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bell and Milam Counties were considered to be in Severe Drought (D2) or worse as defined by the U.S. Drought Monitor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The western portion of Bell County was undergoing Severe Drought (D2).  Oat and wheat products which are now emerging are stressed from the lack of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through South Central Texas early on February 16, 2008 and caused thunderstorms which produced severe hail in eastern portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through South Central Texas early on February 16, 2008 and caused thunderstorms which produced severe hail in eastern portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation developed over parts of northern Texas before sunrise on the 3rd. This precipitation moved over surface temperatures in the upper 20s and lower 30s, which caused it to freeze on contact. Precipitation coverage was not widespread, but enough ice accumulated on roadways that caused numerous traffic accidents. Monetary damages were estimated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous accidents were reported as a result of a thin glaze of ice around Wichita Falls. Monetary damages were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure trough moving across New Mexico and west Texas, aided in the development of high winds across the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains of southwest Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds developed across the Guadalupe Mountains during the late afternoon and into the early evening hours.  The ASOS equipment at Guadalupe Pass measured a peak wind of 71 mph.  The RAWS station at the Pinery measured a peak gust of 59 mph, while the Bowl reported a wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation developed over parts of northern Texas before sunrise on the 3rd. This precipitation moved over surface temperatures in the upper 20s and lower 30s, which caused it to freeze on contact. Precipitation coverage was not widespread, but enough ice accumulated on roadways that caused numerous traffic accidents. Monetary damages were estimated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous accidents were reported as a result of icy roads. Monetary damages were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure trough moving across New Mexico and west Texas, aided in the development of high winds across the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains of southwest Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McDonald Observatory experienced a prolonged high wind event.  Sustained winds of 40-45 mph along with gusts around 60 mph were common throughout the event that began Wednesday afternoon and continued into Thursday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure located over the Four Corners Region of the southwest United States resulted in high winds over the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains of southwest Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were reported at McDonald Observatory during the afternoon on Monday, January 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly moving upper level trough of low pressure, extending across southeast portions of New Mexico and west Texas, aided in the development of high winds across the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains late Wednesday night and during the overnight hours on Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds briefly occurred at the Guadalupe Bowl Wednesday afternoon, with a 60 mph wind gust reported during this time.  High winds were again measured late Wednesday night and early Thursday morning, with a peak wind of 69 mph around midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms produced severe hail across communities north of Houston metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported at the Woodlands Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms passed across the the central County Warning Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter size hail occurred at the intersection of State Highway 242 and Interstate 45.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm passing over Wharton County produced a severe down burst wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A parked 18-wheeler tractor trailer was blown over at the intersection of Highway 59 and FM 441 near Hilje. The driver suffered minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms became better organized and formed a coastal county squall line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees around the town of Brazoria and caused residential roof damage south of Angleton. There was also minor hanger damage reported at the Brazoria County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly moving upper level trough of low pressure, extending across southeast portions of New Mexico and west Texas, aided in the development of high winds across the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains late Wednesday night and during the overnight hours on Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were measured at McDonald Observatory, beginning just prior to midnight, late Wednesday night.  These high winds continued for approximately two hours before gradually diminishing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere, in advance of a series of upper level disturbances located over Arizona and along the West Coast, resulted in widespread high winds over portions of southeast New Mexico and the mountains of southwest Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds across the Guadalupe Mountains lasted in excess of one day, beginning early Monday and concluding Tuesday morning.  A peak wind of 82 mph was reported at Guadalupe Bowl early Tuesday morning.  A peak wind gust of 63 mph was reported at the Pinery on Monday evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere, in advance of a series of upper level disturbances located over Arizona and along the West Coast, resulted in widespread high winds over portions of southeast New Mexico and the mountains of southwest Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at McDonald Observatory from Monday evening through Tuesday afternoon.  Wind gusts of 65-70 mph were consistently measured throughout the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dry line developed over north central Texas on the afternoon of February 25th and began moving eastward in conjunction with an upper level trough moving through the mid-Mississippi Valley.  Moderate lapse rates and lift associated with the dry line helped spur a line of storms in the late afternoon hours over northeast Texas and southwestern Arkansas.  The storms marched eastward throughout the evening and produced hail mainly under 1 inch in many locations across east Texas, southern Arkansas, and northwest Louisiana with some wind damage reported in southern Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell for about two minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough of low pressure moved across eastern Texas and initiated severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 28 foot wall collapsed at a construction site on NASA Road One.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms produced straight line wind damage and penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line wind damage to several residential home roofs and one report of tree damage. Damage also included the collapse of a retail store wall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front traveling through southeast Texas created thunderstorms that producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed power lines and nine large pine trees near the intersection of SH 105 and FM 1484. There was some minor residential home damage from the fallen pine trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system moved across the region, some thunderstorms became severe and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down east of Newton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system produced strong downsloping southwest winds of 35 to 50 mph, temperatures near 90 degrees and relative humidities in the single digits.  These ingredients produced extremely critical fire weather conditions across West Central Texas that resulted in several large wildfires.  A cold front shifted the winds from southwest to north by late afternoon and aided to increase the size of some of the wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 150 acre wildfire developed in the southern part of Callahan County at the intersection of State Highway 36 and County Road 463. Sixty three cars were lost at the local Dodge dealership, three outbuildings and one home were destroyed. Several homes along Highway 36 were evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system propagated eastward over the Southern Plains during the daytime hours of the 25th, and resulted in a few instances of localized and brief high winds over portions of the west Texas South Plains. Damage reports across the area were restricted to the downing of  a single utility line.||The winds combined with a warm and dry airmass, along with an evolving moderate drought, to produce critical fire weather conditions. At least three fire starts were reported across the South Plains, and a significant wind-driven wildfire burned 2,000 acres and destroyed a structure in Hale County. A more damaging wildfire outbreak, however, affected areas of the west Texas Permian Basin and Big Country immediately south of the South Plains region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two gradient severe wind gusts were measured by the Texas Tech University West Texas Mesonet station in Lamb County. A 61 mph gust was initially measured at 11:35 CST at the mesonet site near Amherst. Later, at 14:00 CST, a 62 mph gust was measured near Olton. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire burned approximately two thousand and four hundred acres about two miles southwest of the Buffalo Lake National Wildlife Refuge in southwestern Randall County...or just south of Westline Road near the Randall and Deaf Smith County line. The wildfire started around 1000 CST and was contained around 1800 CST. Four fire departments and also the Texas Forest Service responded to this wildfire. No homes or other structures were damaged or lost and no injuries were reported. The cause of the wildfire was determined to be an escaped control burn of the refuge.

A wildfire burned approximately three hundred acres and started about three miles east southeast of McLean around 1530 CST. The wildfire began just south of the intersection of Interstate 40 and County Road 1...or in extreme southwestern Wheeler County between County Line Road and Farm to Market Road 1442...which is also called County Road 2...just south of Interstate 40. Two homes and five other structures were saved and there were no damage or injuries reported. The wildfire was contained around 2330 CST...and the cause was determined to be from the use of welding equipment.

At least four wildfires occurred in Potter County just north of Amarillo. The largest wildfire started three miles west northwest of Amarillo around 1400 CST west of the intersection of NW 11th Avenue and N Fairmont Street���and just northwest of the intersection of Prospect Street and NW 10th Avenue. This wildfire was later contained near Broadway Drive and St. Francis Avenue around 2100 CST. Immediate evacuations were ordered for the area north of Hastings Avenue���east of Broadway Drive���and south of St. Francis Avenue. About ten to eleven fire departments responded to the wildfire and about eighty firefighters and Texas Forest Service heavy air tankers were used to help contain the wildfire. No injuries were reported. One home and one outbuilding was destroyed. However...one hundred and six homes were saved along with one hundred and thirty-seven other structures. The fire also destroyed fencing. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire initiated along the Glasscock and Sterling County line during the morning.  This wildfire, narrow in size, continued to sweep rapidly east towards Sterling City. This large flame front located on the south side allowed the wildfire to expand and spread into Irion County and ended about 4 miles north of Mertzon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system propagated eastward over the Southern Plains during the daytime hours of the 25th, and resulted in a few instances of localized and brief high winds over portions of the west Texas South Plains. Damage reports across the area were restricted to the downing of  a single utility line.||The winds combined with a warm and dry airmass, along with an evolving moderate drought, to produce critical fire weather conditions. At least three fire starts were reported across the South Plains, and a significant wind-driven wildfire burned 2,000 acres and destroyed a structure in Hale County. A more damaging wildfire outbreak, however, affected areas of the west Texas Permian Basin and Big Country immediately south of the South Plains region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind-driven wildfire was ignited just before 12:00 CST seven miles south of Plainview along Farm to Market Road 400 when a blown tire on a tractor-trailer sparked. The fire quickly spread eastward and threatened nine homes before air assets from the Texas Forest Service were utilized to temporarily contain the blaze around 15:30 CST. A strong northerly wind shift associated with a cold front then caused the briefly contained fire to escape, and the resultant southward moving fire scorched a total of 2,500 acres into the evening hours. A single abandoned structure was destroyed. No injuries were reported, but two journalists were forced to scramble to safety as the shifting fire quickly approached their vehicle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local fire department officials reported that high winds downed a utility line in Lakeview. The downed line sparked a one acre grass fire, but firefighters were able to contain the blaze quickly and prevent a damaging wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and freezing drizzle resulted in localized, but significant, accumulations of ice over portions of the northern South Plains. Weather spotters near Hale Center (Hale County) reported one quarter inch ice accumulations on many surfaces. No damages or injuries were reported.
A weak cold front passed through South Central Texas causing isolated thunderstorms some of which produced severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The general public reported penny size hail near the town of Round Top.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front passed through South Central Texas causing isolated thunderstorms some of which produced severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The general public reported quarter size hail two miles south of La Grange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were dozens of reports of damage due to strong winds as a powerful low pressure system and cold front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down and a truck was blown over in Greenville.  Numerous power outages were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system combined with surface temperatures near the freezing mark resulted in  the development of severe thunderstorms across the Big Country that produced large hail.  At times, the large hail occurred simultaneously with the freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported hail covering the ground.  Law enforcement officials reported vehicle accidents along Interstate 20 as a result of hail covering the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large wildfire started approximately 1900 CST just east of U.S. Highway 83 and between County Road E5 and County Road H...or about three and a half miles northeast of Samnorwood Texas in northeastern Collingsworth County. The wildfire burned about a thousand acres and flared back up the next day. Ten hours were needed to completely contain the wildfire...however no homes or other structures were damaged or lost and no injuries were reported. The cause of the wildfire was determined to be arson.
As a low pressure system moved across the region, some thunderstorms became severe and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 19,389 acre Silver Fire occurred near Robert Lee in Coke County, where about 1500 people were evacuated in the city of Robert Lee. The American Red Cross opened a shelter at the Bronte Elementary School that served approximately 12 people.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 8,900 acre wildfire started near the intersection of FM 137 and CR 204, then moved toward the City of Ozona. However, the fire was contained before reaching Ozona. State Highway 163 and U.S. 90 were closed at the intersections of FM 137 for traffic control.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were dozens of reports of damage due to strong winds as a powerful low pressure system and cold front moved through the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and trees were blown down across the county, causing numerous power outages.  A building in the Bonham town square was damaged when the single-story addition was torn off the back of it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system brought brief windy conditions to the higher elevations of the Davis Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occured for several hours during the early morning hours at Guadalupe Peak. The highest recorded gust was 70 mph at 5:00am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system brought brief windy conditions to the higher elevations of the Davis Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds up to 63 mph were observed in the Davis Mountains just after sunrise. The event lasted for approximately 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a potent upper level storm system that moved into the desert southwest, winds in the mid levels of the atmosphere increased. As a result, high winds occurred over the Guadalupe Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high wind event, lasting 20 hours, produced winds up to 87 mph in the Guadalupe Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southeast winds were established during the proceeding night  bringing abundant low level moisture to the region. By morning, a surface low located just west of Wink had an associated warm front extending northeastward into Oklahoma. Based on the NWS Midland morning upper air balloon sounding, a very unstable airmass was in place. The potential for large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes was realized across the Permain Basin, Western Low Rolling plains, and Trans Pecos Regions as an upper level disturbance interacted with atmospheric instability currently in place across the area. In addition, strong low and mid level wind shear resulted in well organized and long lived thunderstorms. The strongest storms, which produced hail up to softball size and multiple tornadoes, developed as outflow boundaries from thunderstorms north of the area converged with the warm front that became stationary during the afternoon. The severe weather events began by mid morning and lasted into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS damage survey viewed images of a tornado 2.5 miles west southwest of Sparenberg along Farm to Market Road 828. Greg Morgan, local television meteorologist and spotter, provided visual confirmation of this weak tornado lasting approximately one minute. Mr. Morgan and other eyewitnesses estimated a tornado touchdown time of 3:45 pm CDT, and radar imagery confirmed this estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southeast winds were established during the proceeding night  bringing abundant low level moisture to the region. The strongest storms, which produced hail up to softball size and multiple tornadoes, developed as outflow boundaries from thunderstorms north of the area converged with the warm front that became stationary during the afternoon. The severe weather events began by mid morning and lasted into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Members of the Texas Tech Atmospheric Science Group and Greg Morgan from CBS 7 in Odessa witnessed the second tornado, which developed at 3:56 pm CDT, approximately 1.5 miles southwest of Sparenberg over open country, and continued until 4:00 pm. The tornado dissipated approximately 1.75 miles south of Sparenberg, just west of Farm to Market Road 26. No damage was found.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds above 25 mph, with gusts to 40 mph, across the central Lower Rio Grande Valley, occurring within the area between high pressure moving through the Gulf of Mexico and a strengthening trough over west Texas and northeastern Mexico, allowed two fires to spread, including a small fire whose embers caused significant damage to a few structures and threatened hundreds of other buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 400 acre fire, likely spread by gusty southeast winds between 25 and 35 mph, located at the intersection of Farm to Market Road (FM) 649 and Sanchez Road, in western Starr County, was contained. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, along with several local Fire Departments, responded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow moved across north Texas during the early morning hours, dropping anywhere from just a trace to nine inches.  Another area of snow developed across the area that evening as the upper low moved across Texas.  The greatest snow totals were recorded in Decatur, Gainesville, and Sherman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow accumulations froze overnight and caused numerous wrecks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as nine inches of snow fell across the county.  There were about a hundred car wrecks due to icy roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a southward moving cold front on the 29th. Van Zandt and Hopkins Counties had reports of large hail, high winds, and flooding as the boundary wavered in the area on the 30th.  Several supercells and a line of severe storms moved through north Texas on the evening of the 31st as a dry line approached from the west and the warm front remained nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with a fast moving upper level system over the Rocky Mountains brought very windy conditions to the Guadalupe Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An afternoon high wind event in the Guadalupe Mountains resulted in gusts up to 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds in the upper portions of the atmosphere began increasing over the area ahead of an upper level system located near the California/Arizona state line. As this storm system neared the region, increasing mid level winds worked their way down to the surface to produce high winds across the Davis and Guadalupe Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds up to 66 mph occurred during the afternoon and into the late evening hours across the Davis Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing low level winds preceeding and approaching upper level storm system brought abundant moisture to the area. Despite relatively cool surface temperatures, elevated storms were able to develop. Sufficient  instability allowed for several of these storms to become severe resulting in hail, strong winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail was reported by police in Big Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level storm system over northwest Texas triggered a round of strong to severe thunderstorms over northeast Texas, southwest Arkansas, and northwest Louisiana ahead of a cold front that moved east through the area. Isolated tornadoes occurred ahead of a cold front during the morning hours, and there were a few reports of wind damage and flash flooding as the cold front moved east during the daytime.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A daycare on the west side of Houston Street west of downtown near a small creek had to be evacuated due to flash flooding.  Minor street flooding was also reported at Hwy. 59 and Bussey Road and also on Franklin Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported along Farm to Market Road 33.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of increasing drought conditions, strong downslope southwest to westerly winds, and very dry air all contributed to the growth of at least a half dozen wildfires across the west Texas South Plains region during the afternoon and evening hours of the 14th. Four of these wildfires burned thousands of acres and/or resulted in property damage. ||Although wind speeds on the 14th were generally sustained in the 25 to 35 mph range, an isolated severe wind gust of 62 mph was recorded by a Texas Tech University West Texas Mesonet station near Amherst (Lamb County). No wind damage was reported throughout the region. ||Damage resulting from wildfire activity, however, prompted a disaster declaration from the Federal Emergency Management Agency and President George W. Bush. Wildfires on this day scorched more than 135,000 acres across a broad area of the Southern Plains stretching from eastern New Mexico to central Texas. ||The first wildfire to ignite on the west Texas South Plains began around 11:30 CST when a vehicle sparked a fire in thick grasses on an eastern King County ranch. By late afternoon, two large wildfires had merged to account for 19,000 acres of burned rangeland. Texas Forest Service officials reported that the massive wildfire damaged a number of ranch homes and destroyed at least two oil pipelines. Although up to 230 structures were threatened, only one was reportedly destroyed. Portions of U.S. Highway 82 and Texas Highway 222 were closed by local officials as a result of the raging fire. The fire burned out of control for more than twenty-four hours, and then flared up again on multiple occasions during subsequent days. ||A 2,000 acre wildfire started in rough canyon terrain north of U.S. Highway 380, prompting the closure of that highway, in western Kent County around 15:45 CST. The fire was reportedly difficult to suppress due to complex terrain, but no serious damage was reported. The fire remained largely uncontained for approximately two days and also flared up again on a few occasions during subsequent days.||During the evening hours, a large wildfire in Garza County destroyed two trailer homes near Lake Alan Henry. The fire, estimated to have consumed more than 1,200 acres, started around 19:30 CST east of Justiceburg. ||Total damages from South Plains wildfires on the 14th were estimated to be near $250,000.

Strong southeast winds were established during the proceeding night  bringing abundant low level moisture to the region. By morning, a surface low located just west of Wink had an associated warm front extending northeastward into Oklahoma. Based on the NWS Midland morning upper air balloon sounding, a very unstable airmass was in place. The potential for large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes was realized across the Permain Basin, Western Low Rolling plains, and Trans Pecos Regions as an upper level disturbance interacted with atmospheric instability currently in place across the area. In addition, strong low and mid level wind shear resulted in well organized and long lived thunderstorms. The strongest storms, which produced hail up to softball size and multiple tornadoes, developed as outflow boundaries from thunderstorms north of the area converged with the warm front that became stationary during the afternoon. The severe weather events began by mid morning and lasted into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail 19 miles east of Seminole along U.S. Highway 62/180.

A strong upper level system with an associated 120 kt jet stream wind approached the area. The combination of this upper level feature and a deepening low pressure surface low resulted in very windy conditions across the Guadalupe Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were reported across the Guadalupe Mountains during the overnight hours. Peak winds occured around midnight when gusts reached 66 mph at the Guadalupe Bowl.

A cold front moved through the Rio Grande Valley on the 12th triggering a broken line of thunderstorms over portions of the coastal counties of Deep South Texas, forming in Kenedy and eastern Brooks County and propagating south to Cameron County before exiting the coast at South Padre Island. A few thunderstorms produced large hail up to the size of golf balls, and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel to quarter size hail fell in Arroyo City with some of the hail measuring up to 1 inch in diameter. The hail was produced by an isolated thunderstorm which occurred along a cold front moving through Deep South Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Windy and very dry conditions followed a cold front during the afternoon of the 31st.  Sustained northwest winds 20 to 25 mph, gusting up to 35 mph in some locations, combining with relative humidity dropping to 10 percent or lower, increased the likelihood of rapid wild fire spread.  At least two notable fires developed and rapidly spread during the afternoon.  Despite welcome rains during the middle of the month, the western portion of the lower Rio Grande Valley remained in moderate drought at month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire, located on the Jones Ranch, near Agua Nueva, 20 miles south of Farm to Market Road 1017, or approximately 31 miles south of Hebbronville, burned approximately 6,400 acres. An incident command post was established at the site, and numerous local volunteer fire departments assisited in helping contain, and control, the blaze.  No structures were reported damaged, according to reports from the Texas Forest Service.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system combined with surface temperatures near the freezing mark resulted in  the development of severe thunderstorms across the Big Country that produced large hail.  At times, the large hail occurred simultaneously with the freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KTXS TV Station from Abilene reported quarter size hail covering the ground just north of their location on Highway 277.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The husband of a CO-OP observer reported hail fell at their residence near the center of town on Copano Road in Port Mansfield. The hail occurred when an isolated thunderstorm moved through the area. The thunderstorm was triggered by a cold front moving through Deep South Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty southerly winds caused two reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds between 20 and 30 MPH with higher gusts caused a cross arm to break on an electric pole in Gainesville.  A wind advisory was in effect at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty southerly winds caused two reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds between 20 and 30 MPH with higher gusts caused a dead tree to fall, taking a power pole with it.  The tree and the pole blocked traffic on Morrow Avenue in Waco.

Widespread flooding was reported along and south of a northward moving warm front across the lower Toledo Bend and Sam Rayburn country of Deep East Texas into the piney woods of northeast Texas.  These storms trained across the same area for most of the pre-dawn hours of the 30th as well as during the day.  Large hail and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts were reported across portions of the region as well with isolated tornadoes reported across portions of Deep East Texas as well as west central Louisiana during the afternoon hours of March 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado with winds of 65 to 70 mph occurred approximately 1.5 miles southwest of Milam, Texas in Sabine County, Texas along a path approximately 50 yards wide and 2 miles long.  Several large trees were snapped off or uprooted along the path which originated along Dorsey Road extending northeastward across Hwy. 21 before lifting.  Smaller limbs were broken along the path as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to twenty inches of rain fell in the San Augustine and Sabine County area causing record flooding of some local creeks and reservoirs.  Flash flooding was reported throughout the county and many bridges and roads were washed out.  A fatality occurred when two people drove off the side of the road into deeper water.  Only one of the occupants was able to get out.

Generally westerly across the area resulted in the development of a surface trough of low pressure oriented along the Texas panhandle. As this trough strengthened during the day, strong winds through the Davis Mountains resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began in the Davis Mountains at around sunrise and lasted for several hours. Winds reached as high as 69 mph at 9:10am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate southwestly upper level flow and stong mid level winds increased the surface pressure gradient as an upper level system passed to the north of the area. Winds increased in the Davis and Guadalupe Mountains prior to a cold frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were reported across the Guadalupe Mountains. Peak gusts reached 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate southwestly upper level flow and stong mid level winds increased the surface pressure gradient as an upper level system passed to the north of the area. Winds increased in the Davis and Guadalupe Mountains prior to a cold frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short duration high wind event in the Davis Mountains resulted in gusts up to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system traversed eastward across the Texas Panhandle, resulting in very strong winds across the Davis Mountains along with moderate winds through the Guadalupe Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were reported in the Davis Mountains during a high wind event which produced gusts as high as 86 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system traversed eastward across the Texas Panhandle, resulting in very strong winds across the Davis Mountains along with moderate winds through the Guadalupe Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds across the Guadalupe Mountains began around noon and lasted until sunset. The highest gust of 71 mph occurred at 4:14 pm.
